test_cases.scenario_description: A 20-inch natural gas transmission pipeline at 600 psig requires a hot tap to install a new 12-inch branch connection. The pipeline is API 5L X52 with a 0.375-inch wall thickness. The hot tap will be performed using a welding saddle and full encirclement tee. Current flowing conditions are 350 MMSCFD with a temperature of 85°F ambient.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: When performing a hot tap on a high-pressure natural gas transmission pipeline, multiple critical safety requirements must be addressed. First, a detailed hot tap procedure must be developed following API 1104 standards for welding on in-service pipelines. The procedure must include pre-job hazard analysis, isolation planning, and contingency procedures. The pipe wall thickness must be verified using ultrasonic testing at multiple points around the circumference to ensure adequate material remains after accounting for the anticipated weld penetration depth. For API 5L X52 pipe at 600 psig, the minimum wall thickness requirement per ASME B31.8 must be calculated and verified. The maximum allowable pressure during hot tapping must be determined, typically limited to 80% of MAOP during welding operations per company procedures. Preheat requirements must be established based on pipe thickness and ambient temperature; for X52 material at 0.375-inch thickness, preheat to 200-300°F may be required to prevent hydrogen cracking. The welding procedure must use low-hydrogen electrodes and controlled heat input. Before cutting, the pipeline must be purged with inert gas to maintain a safe atmosphere inside the coupon. During the cutting operation, the tapping machine must be monitored for gas detection, and a fire watch must be maintained. After the cut, the coupon must be retained and inspected for proper cutting depth and material integrity. The completed weld must be pressure tested before returning to service, and the branch must be marked appropriately for future reference. All personnel must be certified in hot tap procedures per 49 CFR 192.805 Operator Qualification requirements.
abnormal_variants: Unexpected low wall thickness (0.250-inch vs 0.375-inch nominal)
Detection of hydrogen cracking during weld
Fire in the immediate work area

api_compliance.description: Correct API 1104 and ASME B31.8 compliance

safety_protocols.description: Pre-job planning, hazard analysis, contingency procedures

technical_correctness.description: Proper welding procedures, preheat, and cutting techniques

test_cases.scenario_description: A 911 call reports a strong natural gas odor near a school at 2:45 PM with visible vegetation discoloration in a nearby field. The location is 200 feet from an elementary school with 800 students. Weather conditions show wind at 8 mph from the southwest, temperature 72°F, clear skies. The suspected leak is from a 12-inch distribution main at 60 psig.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Upon receiving a report of a potential gas leak near a school, immediate emergency response procedures per 49 CFR 192.615 must be activated. The first priority is to ensure public safety by evacuating the school if the leak is confirmed or if vapor migration could impact the building. Dispatch should immediately notify the local fire department, police, and school administration. Qualified personnel must be dispatched immediately with appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) including gas detection equipment, fire extinguishers, and emergency response kits. Upon arrival, responders must establish an emergency control zone using the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG) guidelines. For natural gas with wind at 8 mph, initial isolation should extend at least 330 feet in all directions, with consideration for downwind extension to 800 feet or more. Gas detection readings must be taken at multiple points around the school and at HVAC intake locations. If readings exceed 20% LEL in any occupied structure, immediate evacuation is mandatory. The distribution main valve nearest upstream and downstream of the suspected leak must be located and closed to isolate the section. Venting should NOT be performed near the school; instead, gas should be vented at a safe remote location if necessary. All ignition sources must be eliminated within the control zone. Communication must be maintained with emergency responders throughout. A Public Safety Official must be identified to coordinate with first responders. Per 49 CFR 192.615, the operator must also make temporary repairs to return service promptly and schedule permanent repairs. Documentation must include gas readings, actions taken, and notifications made.
abnormal_variants: Ignition source identified within evacuation zone
Wind direction shifts toward school during response

evacuation_protocol.description: Proper evacuation zone establishment and execution

isolation_procedure.description: Correct valve closure and section isolation

public_coordination.description: First responder coordination and public safety

critical_safety_indicators: Evacuation before readings if imminent danger
No ignition sources in hot zone

test_cases.scenario_description: A 42-inch transmission pipeline valve pit requires maintenance on the mainline block valve. The pit is 12 feet deep with a 4-foot diameter access hatch. The valve pit atmosphere was last tested 30 minutes ago and showed normal readings. The valve requires manual operation and inspection of the valve stem packing. Atmospheric monitoring is required per OSHA 1910.146.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Entry into the valve pit constitutes permit-required confined space entry under OSHA 1910.146. Prior to entry, a pre-entry briefing must be conducted covering hazards, entry procedures, communications, and emergency procedures. The atmosphere must be tested before entry with calibrated equipment for oxygen (19.5-23.5%), combustible gas (less than 10% LEL), and toxic gases (H2S less than 10 ppm). Since the last test was 30 minutes ago, the atmosphere must be retested immediately before entry. Continuous atmospheric monitoring must be maintained throughout the entry. A properly trained and equipped attendant must be stationed outside the space with continuous visual and voice communication with the entrant. The attendant must have a pre-planned rescue procedure and be equipped with retrieval equipment including a tripod, winch, and full-body harness. Rescue services must be confirmed available before entry. All isolation and lockout/tagout procedures must be completed for any associated energy sources including electrical, pneumatic, and hydraulic systems. The valve must be locked in position before entry. Ventilation should be employed using explosion-proof blowers to ensure adequate air movement. If the pit shows any atmospheric hazards or if ventilation cannot maintain safe levels, additional controls including supplied air respirators may be required. The entrant must wear appropriate PPE including hard hat, safety glasses, gloves, and steel-toed boots. A confined space entry permit must be completed, signed by the entry supervisor, and posted at the entry point. The permit must include authorized entrants, attendants, entry supervisor, hazards present, controls implemented, and rescue procedures.
abnormal_variants: Oxygen level drops below 19.5% during entry

attendant_requirements.description: Qualified attendant, communication, and rescue equipment

test_cases.scenario_description: A third-party excavator using mechanical equipment has exposed a 6-inch steel gas service line while digging for a swimming pool installation. The excavation is 5 feet deep and has partially undermined the pipe support. The excavator is continuing to dig with a backhoe approximately 10 feet from the exposed pipe. The excavation shows signs of potential cave-in with loose soil walls.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Upon discovery of an exposed gas service line in an active excavation, immediate action must be taken per 49 CFR 192.614 and 192.707 for damage prevention and transmission line marking. The excavator must be instructed to immediately cease all excavation activities within the safety zone. According to API 1102, a safety buffer zone of at least 10 feet must be established on each side of the pipeline, with greater distances if soil conditions warrant. The excavation must be evaluated for cave-in hazards in accordance with OSHA 1926 Subpart P. Given the undermined pipe support, the line is at risk of stress, coating damage, and potential failure. A competent person must evaluate the excavation for protective system requirements including shoring, sloping, or trench shield installation. The exposed pipe must be inspected for damage including coating damage, wall thickness reduction, and structural integrity. A cathodic protection check must be performed to verify the coating system remains intact. If any damage is found, the line must be repaired or replaced per API 1104. The excavation must not be backfilled until the pipe has been properly supported and any damage addressed. Third-party excavator must be educated on the One Call requirements and their responsibilities under 49 CFR 192.614. Documentation must include photographs of the exposed pipe, damage assessment reports, and a close-out inspection confirming proper restoration. If the pipe is found to be damaged, the appropriate emergency response procedures must be activated, potentially requiring service interruption to affected customers.
abnormal_variants: Visible gouge or dent on pipe surface

excavator_control.description: Immediate cessation and safety zone establishment

excavation_safety.description: Competent person evaluation and protective systems

regulatory_compliance.description: One Call and damage prevention compliance

restoration.description: Proper support, repair, and backfilling procedures

test_cases.scenario_description: A transmission pipeline operator needs to isolate a 36-inch mainline ball valve for inspection and stem packing replacement. The valve has not been operated in 5 years. The line section contains approximately 15 MMSCF of natural gas at 900 psig. Closing the valve will isolate a 12-mile section of pipeline containing industrial customers.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Operating a large-diameter transmission line valve that has not been exercised requires careful planning per 49 CFR 192.745. First, the operator must verify valve operability by checking maintenance records for the last valve exercise date. Valves on transmission lines must be exercised at least annually per regulatory requirements. The valve must be closed slowly to prevent water hammer or pressure surges that could damage the pipeline or equipment. Before closing, the operator must notify all affected industrial customers per the established communications plan to allow them time to shut down processes safely. The closure rate should not exceed 1 minute per inch of pipe diameter, meaning the 36-inch valve should take approximately 36 minutes to fully close. During operation, the operator must monitor pressure on both sides of the valve. If significant flow continues past the valve, seat damage may be present. After closure, the valve position must be verified both locally (visual confirmation) and via SCADA. The valve must be fully closed and the handwheel or operator secured to prevent accidental opening. The isolated section must be monitored for pressure buildup which could indicate leakage. Communication with the control center must be maintained throughout the operation. After maintenance is complete, the valve must be reopened slowly following the same rate limitations. The operation must be documented including start/stop times, pressures observed, any anomalies encountered, and customer notifications. If the valve is difficult to operate, it should not be forced; instead, the procedure should be stopped and engineering consulted. Post-maintenance, the valve stem packing must be properly adjusted to prevent leakage while allowing the valve to operate freely.
calculation_requirements: Valve closure rate (1 min per inch)
abnormal_variants: Valve will not turn or is seized
Significant pressure differential across valve during closure

pre_operation_planning.description: Records check, notification, and rate calculation

customer_coordination.description: Notification and communication with affected parties

position_verification.description: Local and SCADA verification of valve status

test_cases.scenario_description: A compressor station operator reports a fire in the lube oil system of Unit 2. The fire is approximately 3 feet in diameter with visible flames and black smoke. The unit is running at full load with 1400 HP output. The fire detection system has activated and the unit is operating in automatic mode. Other personnel are working in adjacent compressor buildings.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Upon detection of a fire in the compressor station lube oil system, immediate action must be taken following the facility emergency action plan per 49 CFR 192.615. The operator must activate the ESD (Emergency Shutdown) system for Unit 2 to stop fuel flow and shut down the compressor. The ESD will isolate fuel gas, close suction and discharge valves, and shut down auxiliary systems. If the ESD does not automatically activate, it must be manually triggered immediately. The fire alarm must be sounded to alert all personnel in the facility. All non-essential personnel must evacuate the compressor building immediately via designated escape routes to the upwind assembly point. The operator must attempt to control the fire using available portable fire extinguishers only if the fire is small (incipient stage) and proper egress is maintained. For a 3-foot diameter lube oil fire, this likely exceeds incipient stage and the operator should evacuate rather than fight the fire. The local fire department must be notified immediately. All other compressor units should be evaluated for safe shutdown if fire spread is possible. The station deluge system or fire water system should be activated if available and appropriate for the fuel type (note: water should NOT be used on lube oil fires unless proper foam or mist systems are in place). Once emergency responders arrive, the operator must brief them on the facility hazards including gas piping, electrical systems, and confined spaces. No attempt should be made to restart or access the unit until cleared by fire officials and company incident commander. The incident must be reported to the DOT through the National Pipeline Mapping System within 30 days if the fire causes damage exceeding $50,000 or results in injury.
calculation_requirements: Fire extinguisher rating vs fire size

fire_response.description: Proper fire fighting decision and notification

critical_safety_indicators: No personnel attempt fire fighting on large fires

test_cases.scenario_description: During pipeline pigging operations, a sour gas release occurs at the pig receiver. The pipeline contains natural gas with 150 ppm H2S concentration. The release point is upwind of the facility control building where 4 operators are working. Portable H2S monitors are reading 45 ppm at the receiver and 12 ppm at the control building entrance. The wind is 10 mph from the receiver toward the control building.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An H2S release during sour gas operations requires immediate response following 49 CFR 192.615 and employer H2S safety procedures. At 45 ppm at the receiver and 12 ppm at the control building, immediate evacuation is required. Personnel must evacuate upwind and crosswind to avoid the vapor cloud. Standard H2S evacuation triggers are: 10 ppm for warning, 20 ppm for respiratory protection, and 100 ppm for immediate evacuation and potential shelter-in-place. All personnel must don emergency escape breathing apparatus (EEBA) if available or evacuate immediately if concentrations exceed safe breathing air. The control room should initiate facility ESD to isolate the pipeline section and stop pig receiver operations. All ventilation intakes to the control building must be shut down to prevent drawing contaminated air inside. Personnel must account for all workers using the mustering system. Any affected personnel showing symptoms of H2S exposure (headache, nausea, respiratory distress) must receive immediate medical attention. The leak must not be approached without proper respiratory protection including supplied air or SCBA units rated for H2S. Wind direction and speed must be monitored as H2S is heavier than air and can accumulate in low-lying areas. Once the release is controlled, entry into the area requires full Level B or C PPE with continuous air monitoring. Proper decontamination procedures must be established for equipment and personnel. The incident must be reported to OSHA and appropriate state environmental agencies if the release exceeds reportable quantities. Medical surveillance must be provided to exposed employees per 29 CFR 1910.1200.
calculation_requirements: PPE selection based on exposure level
abnormal_variants: H2S levels reach 100 ppm at control building
Personnel overcome by H2S in work area
Wind shifts creating new exposure risk

test_cases.scenario_description: A new fiber optic cable installation project has been notified through the One Call system. The proposed crossing is perpendicular to a 20-inch natural gas transmission pipeline at a depth of 4 feet. The crossing location is in agricultural land without existing utilities. The pipeline depth of cover is 36 inches at the crossing point per as-built records.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Responding to a One Call notification for a pipeline crossing requires following procedures outlined in 49 CFR 192.614 for damage prevention. The operator must locate and mark the pipeline within the timeframes specified by state law (typically 48 hours or 2 business days). Marking must follow APWA color code standards with yellow for gas pipelines. The location must include the pipeline centerline and indicate lateral tolerance zone, typically 24 inches on each side per state requirements. Since the proposed fiber crossing is at 4 feet and the pipeline has 36 inches (3 feet) of cover, the vertical clearance is approximately 12 inches. This inadequate clearance requires protection measures including the installation of a concrete barrier or extra padding material above the pipeline. A site meeting must be scheduled with the excavator to review the crossing plan, verify existing utilities with electronic locating equipment, and establish hand-dig requirements within the tolerance zone. Hand-digging or hydro-excavation is required within the tolerance zone to expose the pipe before mechanical excavation proceeds. An on-site observer from the pipeline company may be required during the crossing based on risk assessment. The excavator must be provided with pipeline emergency contact numbers and educated on the requirement to call back if the pipe is encountered at a different depth than marked. After completion, the pipeline operator must inspect the crossing for proper depth restoration, any pipe coating damage, and required pipeline protection. Documentation must include photos of the exposed pipe, installed protection measures, and an as-built of the crossing location. Backfill must be per pipeline company specifications to prevent future settlement and exposure.
abnormal_variants: Pipeline encountered at different depth than marked

locate_response.description: Timely marking and tolerance zone establishment

critical_safety_indicators: No direct mechanical excavation over pipe

test_cases.scenario_description: During a routine patrol, the operator observes an unusual odor and bubbling in a creek crossing. The location is a 16-inch transmission pipeline crossing approximately 50 feet upstream from a residential area. Visual inspection shows discoloration of vegetation on the creek bank. The bubbling appears to be continuous and increasing in frequency.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Observing what appears to be a gas leak at a creek crossing requires immediate action per 49 CFR 192.615 and 192.617 for hazardous leaks. The operator must immediately report the observation to the control room or dispatch and request emergency response. The area must be secured to prevent public access. A minimum evacuation zone of 150 feet in all directions should be established initially, with expansion if gas readings warrant. Gas detection equipment must be used to confirm the presence of natural gas and determine concentration levels. If readings exceed 80% LEL in any area, the evacuation zone must be expanded and ignition sources eliminated. The upstream and downstream block valves for the affected pipeline section must be located and closed to isolate the leak. If the line feeds distribution systems, customer notification and potential service interruption must be coordinated. Once the section is isolated, pressure should be vented safely at a remote location away from the creek and residential area. The creek should be monitored for environmental impact including potential impacts to aquatic life and downstream water users. The state environmental agency and EPA National Response Center must be notified within applicable timeframes if a reportable quantity is reached. Temporary repairs may be made to minimize environmental damage, but permanent repairs must follow API 1104 welding procedures. After repair, the line must be pressure tested before returning to service. The incident must be reported to DOT PHMSA through the Portal within 30 days per 49 CFR 191.5. A root cause analysis must be conducted to determine if the leak resulted from external forces, corrosion, or other mechanisms.
abnormal_variants: Multiple leaks discovered in same area

evacuation_protocol.description: Proper zone establishment and gas monitoring

environmental_response.description: Water quality monitoring and agency notifications

documentation.description: DOT reporting and root cause analysis

test_cases.scenario_description: The compressor station control system indicates a Unit 1 high vibration alarm with subsequent ESD activation. The unit was running at 1200 RPM with 950 psig suction pressure. Post-shutdown, the ESD system status shows: fuel gas valve closed, suction valve closed, discharge valve closed, blowdown valve open. However, the unit vibration sensor remains active.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Following an automatic ESD activation due to high vibration, the operator must verify that the ESD sequence has completed successfully per 49 CFR 192.617 for emergency procedures. The control system indications must be field-verified: fuel gas valve closed and tagged, suction and discharge valves in closed position, blowdown valve open and venting to atmosphere at less than 17% of pipeline pressure within 15 seconds. The compressor must remain in run-down until it reaches zero speed and all rotating energy is dissipated. Personnel must not approach the unit until it is verified that no natural gas is being vented and the vibration source is understood. The blowdown system must be monitored until the unit is fully depressurized. Once safe, maintenance personnel must investigate the high vibration cause which may include bearing failure, coupling misalignment, blade damage, or pipeline-induced vibration. The unit must not be restarted until the root cause is identified and corrected. All ESD activation events must be logged in the station logbook including: time of activation, alarm conditions present, verification of valve positions, and any delays in ESD completion. The event must be reviewed by operations and maintenance management to determine if additional investigation is required. If the vibration was caused by mechanical failure, the unit must be locked out and tagged out for repair. Compressor surge or rotating equipment failure can cause serious injury or damage; therefore, no restart should be attempted without engineering review and management approval. The incident should be evaluated for DOT reportability under 49 CFR 191.5 if the event caused damage exceeding $50,000.
abnormal_variants: Vibration continues to increase after shutdown
Secondary compressor unit fails to start

esd_verification.description: Field verification of all ESD components

investigation.description: Root cause analysis and restart requirements

test_cases.scenario_description: A 30-inch crude oil pipeline requires a hot tap to install a 16-inch branch connection. The pipeline operates at 450 psig and 135°F temperature. The pipe material is API 5L X65 with 0.500-inch wall thickness. The hot tap must be performed on a curve section with 3-degree bend, raising concerns about ovality and stress concentration during welding.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Performing a hot tap on a curved section of high-strength crude oil pipeline requires additional precautions beyond standard hot tap procedures per API 1104 and API 2201. The pipe ovality at the proposed hot tap location must be measured using a pi tape and documented. If the out-of-roundness exceeds 1% of nominal diameter, alternative locations should be considered or the ovality corrected before welding. Due to the X65 material grade, strict preheat requirements must be followed - typically 250°F minimum preheat for this thickness and grade to prevent hydrogen cracking. Interpass temperature must be monitored and maintained below 600°F to prevent metallurgical degradation of the high-strength steel. The welding procedure must be qualified specifically for X65 material and documented in the Welding Procedure Specification (WPS). The maximum allowable pressure during hot tapping must be confirmed - typically limited to 50% of design pressure or as specified in company procedures. A full encirclement tee or split tee must be used to distribute stresses on the curved section. The coupon retention wire must be rated for the higher temperature and oil service. After welding, the joint must be allowed to cool slowly and inspected using ultrasonic testing and magnetic particle examination in addition to standard visual inspection. The completed weld must be pressure tested to 1.25 times MAOP before returning to service. All personnel must demonstrate specific competency for hot tapping high-strength materials and elevated temperature pipelines per 49 CFR 192.805 Operator Qualification.
abnormal_variants: Coupon retention wire fails during cutting

material_compliance.description: X65 material handling and preheat requirements

geometric_considerations.description: Ovality assessment and split tee selection

welding_procedure.description: Proper WPS for high-strength curved section

inspection_requirements.description: UT and MT inspection in addition to visual

test_cases.scenario_description: Emergency repairs are needed on a 36-inch pipeline inside a 10-foot deep manhole vault. The vault has limited ventilation single access hatch. Initial atmospheric testing shows oxygen at 19.8%, LEL at 4%, and H2S at 6 ppm. The repair requires approximately 4 hours of welding work on the pipe coating.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Entry into the manhole vault for extended welding requires full confined space permit procedures per OSHA 1910.146. The initial atmospheric readings (19.8% O2, 4% LEL, 6 ppm H2S) indicate the space is potentially hazardous and requires continuous monitoring. The permit must specify all required controls including forced air ventilation using explosion-proof blowers delivering at least 20 air changes per hour. Welding in a confined space creates additional hazards including toxic fumes, oxygen depletion, and fire risks. A welding fume extraction system must be in place to remove metal fumes and gases produced during welding. The continuous gas monitor must alarm at 19.5% O2, 10% LEL, and 10 ppm H2S with evacuate alarms at 19.5% O2, 20% LEL, and 15 ppm H2S. An attendant must maintain constant visual and voice contact, equipped with emergency retrieval equipment. The space must have a rescue tripod and winch rated for the entry load overhead. A trained rescue team must be on standby given the depth and limited access. Hot work permits are required in addition to the confined space permit, including fire watch, fire extinguisher placement, and weld spark containment. All electrical equipment including welding machines must be properly grounded and protected from the workspace below. Communication equipment including radios in the space must be intrinsically safe for Class I Division 1 areas. Welding leads must be inspected for damage before entry. Entry cannot proceed until all rescue equipment is tested and functional.
abnormal_variants: Attendant loses visual contact with entrant

hot_work_permits.description: Welding permits and fire watch coordination

rescue_preparation.description: Tripod, winch, and standby rescue team

communication.description: Intrinsically safe equipment and contact protocol

test_cases.scenario_description: During a cross-country pipeline replacement project, a 20-foot deep excavation is required in Type C soil near a 500kV overhead transmission line. The excavation will expose a 24-inch natural gas pipeline at the 15-foot depth. Soil conditions are sandy with groundwater seeping into the excavation.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: This excavation presents multiple hazards requiring comprehensive protective systems per OSHA 1926 Subpart P. At 20 feet deep in Type C soil with groundwater, a protective system is mandatory - either trench shield, shoring, or sloping with benching. Simple sloping is not sufficient for saturated Type C soil; a trench box or hydraulic shoring system must be used. The competent person must document the soil classification and protective system design daily. The proximity to the 500kV transmission line requires strict adherence to minimum approach distances per OSHA 1926 Subpart V; generally minimum 10 feet for lines up to 50kV, plus 0.4 inches per kV above 50kV, requiring approximately 16.5 feet clearance for 500kV lines. No equipment may operate within this distance. The excavation requires dewatering to maintain safe working conditions and prevent undermining of the pipe. Dewatering wells or pumps must be placed outside the trench to prevent destabilization. Entry into the excavation requires proper access/egress within 25 feet lateral distance per OSHA requirements, likely requiring a ladder or ramp system. Excavated material must be stored at least 2 feet from the edge to prevent surcharge loading. Atmospheric testing is required before entry due to depth and potential for oxygen displacement by heavier-than-air gases. A spoil pile management plan must address the wet soil conditions. The pipeline exposure requires hand-digging within the tolerance zone and the installation of temporary pipeline support before dewatering to prevent buoyancy or stress issues.
calculation_requirements: Protective system design for Type C soil
abnormal_variants: Trench wall collapse occurs during entry

protective_systems.description: Appropriate trench protection for Type C soil

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline control room receives SCADA alarms showing rapid pressure drop from 950 psig to 400 psig on a 30-mile pipeline section between Block Valve 12 and Block Valve 13. The line contains an estimated 45 MMSCF of gas. Valve 12 shows closed position but Valve 13 indicates partially open. A downstream industrial plant reports pressure fluctuations.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A rapid pressure drop in a pipeline section indicates a potential rupture or major leak requiring immediate emergency response per 49 CFR 192.615. The control room must immediately dispatch field personnel to both valve locations and initiate valve closure procedures if not already complete. The partially open indication on Valve 13 is concerning - local verification of valve position is critical. If Valve 13 is leaking through, it must be manually closed and secured. The pressure drop from 950 to 400 psig represents a significant release; at 400 psig remaining, the leak calculation suggests a large orifice or full rupture. Immediate evacuation of areas downwind must be considered based on gas dispersion modeling. The pipeline section must be isolated at both ends by closing upstream Valve 12 and downstream Valve 13 (if not already closed). All block valves between the points must also be verified closed. Once isolated, the remaining gas in the section must be calculated to determine total release volume. The leak location must be identified using pressure transients, flow modeling, or helicopter patrol. Customer notifications must be made for service interruptions. The area must be secured and gas readings taken to establish safe operating zones. If the leak is confirmed as a rupture, the National Response Center must be notified immediately. DOT reporting requirements under 49 CFR 191.5 apply. The pipeline must not be returned to service until repairs are complete, inspected, and pressure tested. Investigation teams must determine failure mechanism and document contributing factors.
abnormal_variants: Valve 13 cannot be closed manually - seized
Rupture occurs in environmentally sensitive area

isolation_procedure.description: Verification and closure of both block valves

test_cases.scenario_description: A contractor digging without notification strikes a 4-inch cast iron gas main at the street intersection of two high-traffic commercial areas. A 4-inch gash is visible and gas is escaping at high velocity with a hissing sound audible 100 feet away. Traffic is passing through the intersection. The weather is calm with 5 mph wind.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A high-pressure gas leak from a damaged main in a commercial intersection requires immediate life-safety response per 49 CFR 192.615 and 192.617. The first priority is securing the area and eliminating ignition sources. The operator must immediately block off the intersection and adjacent streets to vehicular and pedestrian traffic. A minimum exclusion zone of 200 feet in all directions should be established immediately and expanded based on gas readings. No vehicles or equipment should be allowed within the zone. Personnel must not enter the immediate area without respiratory protection if gas concentrations exceed IDLH levels; initial approach should be from upwind with continuous gas monitoring. The struck gas line must be isolated by closing the nearest upstream and downstream valves. For cast iron pipe, valve closure must be done slowly to prevent additional damage from water hammer. Once isolated, the remaining gas must be safely vented at a remote location. Police and fire department must be notified for traffic control and public safety. If gas concentrations reach 20% LEL in any structure, immediate evacuation of those structures is required. The contractor must be removed from the area and secured for investigation. Documentary evidence including photos of the damage and excavation site must be collected per 49 CFR 192.614 damage investigation requirements. Service restoration requires proper repair of the cast iron pipe, typically requiring replacement with steel or polyethylene, and pressure testing before return to service. The incident must be reported to DOT and the One Call notification center. If the damage was caused by failure to notify, enforcement action against the contractor may be pursued.

public_safety.description: Building evacuation and ignition source control

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline patrol discovers construction equipment working within 20 feet of a 36-inch high-pressure natural gas pipeline right-of-way. The equipment is excavating foundations for a commercial building. No One Call tickets are on file for the address. The operator is off-site and not responding to attempts to contact.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Unauthorized excavation near a high-pressure pipeline represents an imminent hazard to public safety per 49 CFR 192.614 and 49 CFR 192.707. Immediate action must stop the excavation before pipeline contact occurs. The patrol must approach the equipment operator or construction foreman if present and instruct them to immediately cease excavation. The pipeline location must be marked visibly with yellow flags or paint extending at least 10 feet on each side of the pipe centerline. Local law enforcement should be contacted if personnel cannot be located or will not comply with the stop-work order. State One Call laws typically provide for civil and criminal penalties for unauthorized excavation - the excavator must be educated on these requirements and their notification obligation. Photographs must be taken documenting the excavation location relative to the pipeline, equipment being used, and current depth. The pipeline company must locate and mark the precise pipe location using electronic locating equipment. If excavation has already begun, a hand-dig inspection must be performed to verify pipe integrity and depth of cover. The operator must issue a stop-work letter to the property owner and excavator. All costs associated with response, inspection, and remediation must be billed to the responsible party. If the excavator continues after being notified, the matter should be referred to the state underground damage prevention authority for enforcement action. The incident must be documented for potential DOT reporting if excavation activity constitutes an encroachment or potential integrity threat.
abnormal_variants: Pipe already contacted but not damaged
Construction supervisor claims proper notification was made

notification_enforcement.description: One Call education and law enforcement if needed

test_cases.scenario_description: A new apprentice operator is assigned to perform leak investigation and pinpointing using combustible gas indicators. The apprentice has completed classroom training but has not yet been evaluated in the field. Two experienced technicians are available - one OQ qualified in leak investigation but not in evaluation, and another qualified as an OQ evaluator but not in leak investigation procedures.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Per 49 CFR 192.805 and Subpart N, pipeline operators must verify that individuals performing covered tasks are qualified. A trainee performing leak investigations must be under the direction and observation of a qualified individual who is OQ certified for both the task AND evaluation. In this scenario, the two available personnel must work together: the OQ-evaluated individual for leak investigation can perform the task while the OQ evaluator observes and evaluates the apprentice performance. Neither technician alone can fulfill both roles. The apprentice cannot perform leak investigation unsupervised until achieving OQ certification. The evaluation must cover both the technical skills (using gas detection equipment, interpretation of readings, leak pinpointing techniques, documentation) and the safety protocols (hazard recognition, PPE use, emergency response). The evaluation must be documented on the individual's OQ record with specific tasks evaluated, date completed, and evaluator signature. If no qualified evaluator is present, the apprentice must perform non-covered tasks only until proper evaluation can be arranged. The company must maintain OQ records for all covered tasks per 49 CFR 192.809 and make them available for DOT inspection. Failure to properly evaluate personnel can result in DOT enforcement action and fines. The apprentice should shadow qualified technicians and practice with training equipment until ready for formal evaluation.
abnormal_variants: Supervisor instructs apprentice to work alone
Apprentice has expired OQ for similar task

test_cases.scenario_description: A grass fire has ignited near a pipeline valve station during drought conditions. The fire front is 200 feet from the valve station and moving toward the facility at 10 mph with the wind. The valve station contains multiple motor-operated valves, electrical panels, and a small control building. There are no personnel currently on site.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A wildfire approaching a pipeline valve station requires immediate protective action following the facility emergency action plan per 49 CFR 192.615 and NFPA standards for wildland fire interface. The control center must immediately dispatch personnel to assess the situation and prepare for facility protection or shutdown. Local fire authorities must be notified of the hazard and the location of the valve station. If the fire is within an unsafe distance (typically 100 feet), an orderly shutdown of the valve station systems should be initiated remotely to de-energize electrical equipment and reduce ignition risks. Motor-operated valves should be positioned to known safe positions (usually closed). The control building and equipment must be evaluated for shelter-in-place or active protection options. Available fire suppression resources including portable pumps, sprinkler systems, or fire retardant foam should be deployed. Clearing vegetation around critical equipment using available personnel or contractor resources may create defensible space. If evacuation is necessary, personnel must retreat upwind and uphill from the fire path. The fire service should be advised of the natural gas hazard and station contents including isolation valve locations to aid in response planning. After the fire passes, the facility must be inspected for damage to valves, actuators, electrical systems, and control equipment before returning to operation. Any damage to protective coatings or equipment must be repaired. Emergency responders should be alerted to remain clear of the area until all systems are verified safe.
abnormal_variants: Fire blocks road access to station
Remote shutdown fails - requires manual operation
Fire reaches station before evacuation complete

immediate_response.description: Dispatch, fire service notification, and assessment

test_cases.scenario_description: An operator is performing routine maintenance on a separator vessel in a sour gas facility. The facility processes gas containing 500 ppm H2S. While removing a pressure transmitter, a gasket failure occurs releasing gas directly into the operator's face. The operator is 15 feet from the nearest emergency exit and is not wearing respiratory protection.
test_cases.expected_response_detified: An uncontrolled release of gas containing 500 ppm H2S directly onto an unprotected worker is immediately life-threatening per OSHA and API RP 55. At 500 ppm, H2S can cause unconsciousness within minutes and death within 1-3 breaths. The operator must immediately evacuate using available escape breathing apparatus (EEBA) or by holding breath while moving to fresh air. If an EEBA is not immediately available, the best course is move upwind immediately while holding breath or taking shallow breaths. The operator should activate the nearest emergency shower/eyewash station if chemical exposure to skin or eyes occurred. The facility ESD should be activated to isolate the separator. Other personnel in the area must evacuate immediately upwind. The affected individual requires immediate medical attention - H2S exposure can cause delayed pulmonary edema even if the individual initially feels recovered. Oxygen must be administered if available. The incident must be reported as a serious injury to OSHA within 8 hours. For H2S concentrations above 100 ppm, the area must be evacuated and entry prohibited without SCBA or supplied air respirators. The facility must investigate why the operator was performing maintenance on a live system without proper isolation and draining procedures. Standard practice requires double block and bleed isolation or complete vessel depressurization before removing pressurized components. The response must include reviewing lockout/tagout procedures and ensuring future maintenance follows vessel entry and servicing safety protocols per OSHA 1910.147 and 1910.146.
abnormal_variants: Second operator also exposed while attempting rescue

immediate_escape.description: Hold breath and evacuate to fresh air

rescue_protocols.description: Rescuer safety - no entry without SCBA

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline pressure control system fails resulting in over-pressure of a distribution system. The SCADA shows supply pressure of 120 psig exceeding the regulator station setpoint of 60 psig. Downstream low-pressure alarms are triggering and multiple residential customers report appliance issues and high flames.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An over-pressure event in a distribution system is a serious safety hazard per 49 CFR 192.195 and 192.617. At double the normal operating pressure, appliance regulators may fail and cause gas leaks or fires. The control center must immediately identify the cause of the over-pressure - typically a stuck high-pressure regulator, failed monitor regulator, or bypass malfunction. The supply valve to the affected station must be closed or the bypass blocked to stop the over-pressure condition. If the station has an over-pressure protection device (OPSO or slam-shut), it should trip to isolate the station. Once the supply is isolated, system pressure must be monitored to ensure it bleeds down to normal levels. Affected customers must be notified and their service shut off at the meter until inspection can verify safe operating conditions. Customer appliances must be checked for over-pressure damage including cracked heat exchangers, damaged appliance regulators, and pilot safety failures. The cause of the over-pressure must be investigated before returning to service - components may include regulator failure, diaphragm rupture, sensing line issues, or maintenance errors. The station equipment must be tested, repaired, and verified before reopening. DOT reporting may be required if the over-pressure exceeds equipment ratings or causes damage. All affected customers must have service restored only after appliance inspection by qualified personnel. The incident requires a comprehensive post-incident review to identify system weaknesses and implement corrective actions including additional over-pressure protection devices.
abnormal_variants: OPSO fails to trip despite over-pressure

test_cases.scenario_description: A 42-inch refined products pipeline at 350 psig requires hot tapping to install a new scraper trap. The pipeline is 0.625-inch wall thickness, API 5L X60 material, and contains diesel fuel. The hot tap location is adjacent to a highway under a bridge, creating confined work area and traffic control requirements.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping a refined products pipeline requires special procedures per API 1104 and API 2201 with additional considerations for flammable liquids and confined work areas. The diesel fuel presents fire and explosion hazards different from natural gas - welding near flammable liquids requires additional precautions including proper ventilation, fire watch, and spill containment. A Job Hazard Analysis must address work under a bridge including traffic control, overhead clearance, and bridge structural protection. Traffic control must follow DOT Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices (MUTCD) standards with advance warning signs, cones, and flaggers. The X60 material requires preheat to 250°F minimum for 0.625-inch thickness. The coupon cutter must be selected for liquid service - refined products require seal compatibility and spark containment. The area must have fire extinguishers rated for Class B flammable liquids (minimum 20-B rating) positioned at 30-foot intervals. A welding blanket or fire-resistant tarp must be placed under the work area to catch sparks. The pipeline should be isolated upstream and downstream if possible to stop flow, or flow minimized to reduce heat dissipation during welding. Atmospheric testing before welding must confirm LEL below 10% in the work area; since diesel vapors are heavier than air, testing must include low-lying areas under the bridge. The hot tap machine must be bonded and grounded to prevent static electricity. After the cut, liquid must be contained and properly disposed per EPA regulations. Post-weld heat treatment may be required for X60 material. The completed weld requires volumetric inspection (RT or UT) and pressure testing to 1.5 times MAOP.
abnormal_variants: Diesel leak discovered at proposed hot tap location

traffic_safety.description: MUTCD compliance and work zone safety

fire_protection.description: Class B extinguisher placement and fire watch

test_cases.scenario_description: Maintenance is required on a pig receiver barrel that has just completed a batch of sour gas pigs. The barrel is 36-inch diameter by 20 feet long with single entry hatch. Initial readings show O2 at 20.1%, LEL at 0%, and H2S at 8 ppm. The pig receiver must be opened to remove debris and inspect the seals.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Working on a sour gas pig receiver requires careful procedure following OSHA 1910.146 for confined space and API RP 55 for H2S safety. At 8 ppm H2S, the space is above the OSHA 10-hour TWA but below the 15 ppm STEL; entry is permitted with continuous monitoring and PPE ready. The pig receiver must be fully isolated using double block and bleed isolation before entry - this includes closing inlet and outlet valves plus the bypass line. The receiver must be depressurized through the blowdown valve and confirmed at atmospheric pressure via pressure gauges locally and at SCADA. The pig indicator must show the pig removed before opening the closure. The quick-opening closure must be operated per manufacturer procedures - typically requiring partial opening to vent any trapped pressure, then full opening. After opening, the atmosphere must be retested at multiple points inside the receiver barrel including the bottom where H2S may concentrate. Ventilation must be established using forced air blowers. Continuous atmospheric monitoring is required with alarms at 10 ppm H2S (warning) and evacuation at 15 ppm H2S. Rescue equipment including tripod and winch must be positioned at the entry point. The attendant must maintain visual and voice contact. The work scope includes removing debris which may be pyrophoric (iron sulfide residue from sour gas) and requires wetting before handling. PPE includes full-face respirator with H2S cartridges available at 10 ppm, switching to supplied air if concentrations rise. Entry cannot proceed without full isolation verification and rescue team availability.
abnormal_variants: H2S spike to 25 ppm when closure is opened
Pyrophoric debris ignites upon air contact
Quick-opening closure will not seal after inspection

test_cases.scenario_description: A 24-inch transmission pipeline requires excavation to address a potential coating anomaly identified by inline inspection. The excavation must be 30 feet long and 8 feet deep in a congested urban area with underground utilities and reinforced concrete sidewalk above. The soil is Type B clay with potential for rock at depth.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Excavating in an urban utility corridor requires careful planning following OSHA 1926 Subpart P and damage prevention procedures per 49 CFR 192.614. Before excavation, all utilities must be located and marked using electronic locating and potholing. The One Call ticket must be confirmed with all utility responses documented. Given the 30-foot length and 8-foot depth in Type B soil, hydro-excavation or vacuum excavation is recommended to safely expose the pipeline without mechanical damage. If mechanical excavation is used, a protective system is required - trench shields or shoring must be specified. The competent person must evaluate soil conditions daily. Atmospheric testing is required before entry due to depth and potential utility crossings. Traffic control must be established with lane closures following local permitting requirements. The sidewalk load creates surcharge conditions requiring additional protective system design. Spoil must be stored at least 2 feet from the edge. Access/egress must be provided within 25 feet. Rock excavation requires special procedures if encountered including the use of non-mechanical methods like hydraulic splitters or air tools within the tolerance zone. Hand-digging is required within 24 inches horizontally of the pipeline per API 1102. The pipeline must be exposed carefully to avoid coating damage. Pipe supports may be required to prevent stress if large sections are exposed. After inspection/repair, proper backfill compaction is required to prevent settlement and future coating damage.
calculation_requirements: Protective system for Type B soil

traffic_control.description: Lane closures and urban work setup

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline maintenance project requires closing four block valves in series to isolate a 50-mile section of 30-inch transmission pipeline. The valves are spaced 12 miles apart. The valves have not been operated in 3 years and some show partial closure indications on SCADA.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Isolating a long pipeline segment requires systematic valve operation per 49 CFR 192.745 and 192.747. Before operation, maintenance records must be reviewed for last exercise dates and any known issues. Valves showing partial closure must be evaluated - if they were not intentionally partially closed, this may indicate valve seat issues, debris, or operator error. The sequence of operation must be planned: typically starting with the downstream valve to minimize pressure differential across remaining valves during closure. However, if full isolation is required, the plan must coordinate closure timing with the control room. Before operating, customer notifications must be made for service interruption. Each valve must be operated at controlled rate - typically 1 minute per inch of diameter, meaning 30 minutes per valve for 30-inch valves. During operation, differential pressure across each valve must not exceed design limits; if pressure differentials are high, valves may need to be closed in parallel or with flow balancing. After closure, each valve position must be verified locally (visual inspection) and confirmed at SCADA. If any valve shows leakage, additional closures may be needed or the line may require venting before work. The isolated section must be monitored for pressure changes indicating leaks. Valve handwheels must be secured after closure. Documentation must include actual closure times, pressures before/after, valve condition observed, and any anomalies. If valves cannot be fully closed or operated, alternate isolation points must be identified. Post-operation, valves must be exercised to maintain operability.
abnormal_variants: First valve only closes 80% and leaks significantly
Valve operator shears when attempting closure

documentation.description: Records of closure times and conditions

test_cases.scenario_description: A major hurricane is approaching the pipeline corridor. Current predictions show Category 3 winds and storm surge of 8 feet affecting coastal facilities including valve stations, compressor stations, and offshore platforms. The storm is 24 hours from landfall.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hurricane preparation requires activating the Emergency Action Plan per 49 CFR 192.615 48-72 hours before projected landfall. Facility prioritization must occur - identify critical infrastructure requiring protection versus facilities that must be shut down. Coastal facilities potentially subject to storm surge must be evaluated for flooding risk. Personnel safety is the primary concern; evacuation of non-essential personnel should begin before mandatory evacuation orders. At 24 hours, final shutdown and securing of facilities must occur. Compressor stations should be shut down and depressurized. Block valves should be positioned to minimize potential damage and isolate sections if rupture occurs. Tankage must be secured and potential floating debris cleared. Electrical systems should be de-energized to prevent damage and electrocution hazards. Backup generators should be fueled and tested. Records and emergency equipment should be moved to secure locations above projected flood levels. Communication systems must be verified including satellite phones for post-storm damage assessment. Coordination with local emergency management is essential to understand evacuation routes and shelter locations. After the storm passes, a damage assessment team must be deployed using established protocols. Personnel should not approach flooded facilities until electrical safety can be verified. Leak detection surveys must be conducted before system restart. Any damage to coating, supports, valves, or structures must be repaired before return to service. Storm documentation must be maintained for regulatory reporting and insurance claims.
abnormal_variants: Storm intensifies to Category 4 with 6 hours notice
Offshore platform loses communication before securing

test_cases.scenario_description: During a routine patrol, the operator discovers a significant gas leak from a unit cooler on Compressor Unit 3. Gas is visible venting at high velocity and an audible hiss indicates substantial pressure. The unit is currently running at 80% load with suction pressure of 850 psig.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A high-pressure gas leak from a compressor cooler is a serious hazard requiring immediate response per 49 CFR 192.615 and 192.617. The operator must immediately activate the compressor ESD system to isolate fuel gas, close suction and discharge valves, and initiate blowdown. If the ESD does not function automatically, manual ESD buttons at the unit or control room must be activated. Personnel must immediately evacuate the area moving upwind to avoid the gas cloud. A wind direction indicator should be consulted or observed (smoke, flags) to determine safe evacuation routes. The area must be secured to prevent personnel entry. Ignition sources must be eliminated including shutting down nearby equipment if safe to do so, and prohibiting vehicle traffic or electrical switching. Fire department must be notified. Gas detection must be used to establish hot zones - evacuate all personnel if readings exceed 20% LEL. If the leak source can be safely identified and isolated (such as closing an isolation valve on the cooler circuit), this may limit the release volume. However, personnel should not approach the leak to attempt manual isolation unless wearing SCBA and protective gear. Station deluge systems should be activated if available. The gas release must be reported to DOT if it exceeds reportable thresholds per 49 CFR 191.5. After the unit is fully depressurized and the area is safe, maintenance must inspect the cooler for damage, determine the leak cause, and repair before restart. The incident must be investigated including root cause analysis. Restart requires management approval and verification of all safety systems.
abnormal_variants: ESD fails to trip - manual isolation required
Gas cloud drifts toward control building

test_cases.scenario_description: A lightning strike has ignited the methane vent stack at a compressor station. A 20-foot flame is visible with black smoke. The lightning storm is still active in the area. The vent stack serves multiple seal gas vents and the station blowdown system.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A methane vent stack fire requires immediate evaluation following the facility Emergency Action Plan per 49 CFR 192.615. While vent stack fires may self-extinguish, active flames indicate significant combustion that must be evaluated for spread potential. The control room must be notified immediately. Personnel must remain clear of the vent stack due to radiant heat and potential flashback. The lightning storm still active increases electrical hazard - no personnel should be on elevated surfaces or near conductive structures. If the fire is limited to the vent stack and the stack is rated for flame impingement, the fire may be monitored rather than extinguished. Attempting to extinguish a gas vent fire may cause more dangerous conditions including unburned gas accumulation and explosion risk. The station ESD should be activated if not already done to isolate sources feeding the vent. Gas flow to the vent should be stopped at the source if the system design allows - this may mean shutting down compressors and closing isolation valves. Fire department should be notified but advised that extinguishing may not be appropriate. Water should NOT be used on gas fires unless necessary to cool nearby structures. Personnel must account for all station staff. After the storm passes and the fire is out, the vent stack must be inspected for damage before returning to service. The incident must be documented including photographs and reporting to DOT if damage thresholds are reached per 49 CFR 191.5.
abnormal_variants: Fire spreads to nearby electrical equipment
Seal gas continues to feed fire after compressor shutdown

test_cases.scenario_description: A wellhead flange leak at a gas processing plant has released sour gas containing 2% H2S. The release rate is estimated at 50 MMSCFD. The facility is upwind of a residential area approximately 0.5 miles away. Current wind is 12 mph toward the residential area.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A large H2S release from a gas processing plant is a major emergency requiring immediate activation of the facility Emergency Response Plan per 49 CFR 192.615 and API RP 55. At 2% H2S (20,000 ppm), this is immediately dangerous to life and health. Immediate evacuation of downwind areas must be initiated. The facility general alarm must be activated and shelter-in-place ordered for upwind areas. Emergency services must be notified including fire, police, and emergency management for the residential area. The well must be shut in and isolated at the wellhead if safe to do so - however, personnel should not approach the leak without SCBA. Remote isolation valves should be operated from the control room. Facility ESD should isolate all process equipment. Wind direction and speed must be monitored continuously - if wind shifts, evacuation zones must be adjusted. Air monitoring must be established downwind to track the gas cloud. Emergency responders should be provided with H2S safety cards (from ERG or safety data sheets) showing exposure limits and symptoms. Medical facilities should be alerted for potential casualty treatment. Public warning systems including sirens, reverse 911, or mobile alerts should be activated. The release must be reported to the National Response Center and state environmental agencies immediately. After the release is stopped, entry for assessment requires full SCBA and buddy teams. Atmospheric monitoring must continue until H2S levels are below 10 ppm. The root cause of the flange failure must be investigated. Emergency exercises should be conducted after the incident to improve response.
abnormal_variants: Wind shifts toward alternate population center
Wellhead shutoff valve fails to close

isolation.description: Remote well shut-in and process ESD

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline operator receives SCADA alerts showing a pipeline section cooling from 85°F to 45°F over 2 hours while pressure remains constant at 900 psig. The pipeline contains natural gas with known hydrate formation conditions at 50°F at this pressure. The line passes through a swampy area with poor drainage.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Rapid pipeline cooling approaching hydrate formation conditions requires immediate intervention per 49 CFR 192.617 for abnormal operation response. Hydrates can form ice-like crystals that block flow and damage equipment. The control room must immediately investigate the temperature drop cause - possible causes include: injection of cold gas from upstream, ambient cooling (weather event), Joule-Thomson cooling from pressure reduction, or instrument malfunction. If the pipeline is indeed approaching 50°F at 900 psig, immediate action must prevent hydrate formation. Options include: injecting methanol or glycol to lower hydrate formation temperature, reducing pressure to shift hydrate formation conditions, or heating the gas. Since pressure is constant, methanol injection is the typical response. Methanol must be injected at a concentration sufficient to lower the hydrate formation point below operating temperature - typically requiring 20-40% methanol concentration in the water phase. If methanol is not available, pressure reduction may be considered but must be coordinated with customers. The line should not be shut down as stagnant cold gas may accelerate hydrate formation. If hydrates have already formed, the line may need to be depressurized and warmed. After the incident, investigation must determine the root cause of the temperature drop to prevent recurrence. Cold weather operating procedures should be reviewed. SCADA temperature alarms should be evaluated and potentially adjusted. Methanol inventory should be checked and replenished.
abnormal_variants: Hydrates form and block line before methanol available
Temperature drop caused by major upstream rupture

test_cases.scenario_description: A landowner has installed a private water well within 15 feet of a 12-inch high-pressure natural gas pipeline. The well was installed without notification to the pipeline operator. The wellhead and electrical pump are now in place. No excavation was visible and the installation method is unknown.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An unreported well installation near a pipeline is an encroachment requiring immediate assessment per 49 CFR 192.614 and integrity management requirements. The operator must dispatch personnel to locate the pipeline and assess the well location relative to the pipe centerline. The depth and installation method must be determined - if directional drilling was used, there is high risk of pipeline contact. Ground-disturbing activities within the pipeline right-of-way typically require notification under One Call laws. Since the work is complete, a post-construction investigation is required including hand-digging to expose the pipe and verify no damage occurred. The well location within 15 feet likely violates standard setback requirements; many operators require 25-50 foot setbacks for water wells. The landowner must be contacted to educate them on notification requirements and potential safety hazards. If the well is within the standard setback, the operator may require relocation or additional protective measures. Cathodic protection readings must be taken to verify coating integrity and continuing protection. Any coating damage or pipe deformation discovered must be repaired per API 1104. The incident must be documented with photos, measurements, and correspondence. If the well presents an integrity threat, the operator may need to file a DOT reportable event per 49 CFR 191.5. Legal counsel may be involved to address right-of-way violations. If the landowner refuses to cooperate, the matter may be escalated to state public utility commission or courts.
abnormal_variants: Excavation exposes coating damage from well installation
Well is directional drilled under the pipeline

test_cases.scenario_description: A 16-inch pipeline containing wet sour gas (100 ppm H2S) requires hot tapping to install a new pressure monitor. The line operates at 600 psig and has 0.375-inch wall thickness of API 5L X52 material. The wet gas contains significant water vapor and potentially small amounts of methanol from upstream injection.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping a sour gas pipeline requires specialized procedures per API 1104, NACE MR0175/ISO 15156 for sour service, and company procedures for H2S exposure. At 100 ppm H2S, respiratory protection is required for any work on the line. Personnel must wear full-face respirators with H2S cartridges or supplied air respirators during the operation. Continuous atmospheric monitoring is required with alarms at 10 ppm H2S. The line must be analyzed for wet gas service effects - water and methanol can cause hydrogen charging of the steel, requiring careful preheat control to prevent hydrogen cracking. The welding procedure must use low-hydrogen electrodes and strictly controlled heat input. Preheat must be maintained at 250°F throughout the weld. The maximum interpass temperature for X52 in sour service must not exceed 500°F. The coupon cutter must be compatible with sour service and properly cleaned after use to prevent sulfide stress cracking of components. The hot tap area must have emergency showers and eyewash stations given the H2S and potential caustic exposure if methanol is present. All personnel must be H2S trained and emergency rescue procedures must be in place. Sulfide stress cracking (SSC) risk requires material verification - the parent pipe and hot tap tee must be verified as SSC resistant per NACE MR0175. Post-weld inspection must include hardness testing to verify the HAZ is below 22 HRC to prevent SSC. The completed weld must be pressure tested before return to service. Entry into the hot tap machine area may require confined space procedures depending on configuration.
abnormal_variants: H2S spike to 200 ppm during welding
Hardness test shows 25 HRC in HAZ

test_cases.scenario_description: An operator needs to enter a meter station building to replace a filter element on the inlet line. The building is 20x30 feet with 12-foot ceiling and single personnel door. The building houses multiple pressure regulators, filters, and relief valves. Initial readings show O2 at 20.4%, LEL at 2%, and CO at 15 ppm.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Entry into a natural gas meter station building may constitute confined space entry depending on atmospheric conditions and configuration per OSHA 1910.146. At 2% LEL and 15 ppm CO, the space requires evaluation - 15 ppm CO is at the OSHA 8-hour TWA limit and requires monitoring. Although single-door buildings may not be permit-required confined spaces by configuration, hazardous atmospheres trigger the standard. Entry requires a pre-entry atmospheric test using calibrated equipment. Continuous monitoring is required for LEL, O2, and CO with data-logging capability. Natural or mechanical ventilation must be verified as operational before entry. A building entry permit should document the scope of work, atmospheric conditions, communication methods, and emergency procedures. A second person (safety watch) should be stationed outside or in communication via radio given the CO levels. Entry should be limited to task duration and personnel should exit if CO alarms activate. PPE must include personal gas monitor with alarm functions. Tooling must be non-sparking given the 2% LEL. The work scope involves filter element replacement which may release trapped gas requiring additional precautions. The inlet isolation valve should be closed and locked to prevent gas ingress during work. After isolation, the filter housing must be vented to safe atmosphere before opening. The used filter element contains hydrocarbon residue and must be handled as hazardous waste per EPA regulations. Entry cannot proceed if ventilation fails or CO levels rise above 25 ppm. Post-entry atmospheric conditions must be documented.
abnormal_variants: CO rises to 50 ppm during filter change

test_cases.scenario_description: A horizontal directional drilling (HDD) project for a fiber optic cable needs to cross under a 36-inch natural gas transmission pipeline at 45 feet depth. The HDD will pass 8 feet below the pipe. The pipeline is 48 inches deep and has cathodic protection. The bore path is parallel to the pipeline for 120 feet.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Horizontal directional drilling under a transmission pipeline requires special crossing procedures per 49 CFR 192.614 and PHMSA Advisory Bulletins. HDD presents unique hazards including potential frac-outs, bore path deviation, and loss of drilling fluid that could damage the pipeline coating or expose the pipe. A pre-construction meeting with all stakeholders is essential to review the bore plan and establish hand-dig verification requirements. The HDD alignment should be perpendicular to the pipeline where possible; the parallel alignment requires additional monitoring. Paralleling for 120 feet increases risk of intersection if bore path deviates. The 8-foot clearance below the pipe is acceptable but requires consideration of buoyancy and settlement effects from the HDD installation. The crossing must be hand-dug to expose the pipe before HDD proceeds. A guide trench should be excavated to the planned bore depth to verify pipe location and ensure no utilities are within the bore path. Drilling fluid must be monitored for pressure buildup that could heave the ground and stress the pipe. If frac-out occurs, drilling must stop immediately. The cathodic protection system must be monitored during HDD to verify no damage to the rectifier or anode bed. Post-installation, the pipeline must be exposed at the crossing location to verify depth of cover, coating integrity, and no damage from drilling fluids or equipment. As-built drawings must be submitted showing the actual HDD path relative to the pipeline. Damage to coating requires immediate repair per API 1160. If bore path deviated and contacted the pipe, the incident must be reported to DOT per 49 CFR 191.5.
abnormal_variants: Drilling fluid frac-out occurs near pipeline
Bore path deviates upward toward pipe
Coating damage discovered during post-drill exposure

pre_construction.description: Stakeholder meeting and bore plan review

monitoring.description: Drilling fluid pressure and bore tracking

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline operator needs to close a 48-inch mainline valve with a motor-operated valve (MOV) actuator. The MOV has just undergone maintenance on the electric motor and gear train. The valve controls flow of 1200 MMSCFD at 950 psig. The operator station is remote from the control center.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Operating a large MOV after maintenance requires post-maintenance verification before full operation per 49 CFR 192.745 and manufacturer procedures. The maintenance work must be documented including torque settings, limit switch adjustments, and test results. Before full operation, the MOV should be cycled partially to verify proper function - this can be done using the local control station in jog/inching mode. The valve should be operated incrementally (10-20% of full stroke) while monitoring motor current, valve position feedback, and torque indicators. Abnormal current draw or torque readings indicate potential issues requiring investigation before full operation. During closure, the differential pressure across the valve must be monitored - closing against high differential pressure can damage the valve seat or actuator. If possible, close against minimal flow by coordinating with upstream flow control. The closure rate must be controlled to prevent water hammer - for large valves this typically means 30-60 seconds to close rather than rapid closure. Local position indicators must be monitored to verify actual valve position matches the control system indication. If remote and local indications disagree, verify at the valve using the local stem position indicator. After closure, leakage through the valve should be monitored using pressure gauges on both sides or acoustic leak detection. The valve torque and limit switches should be recalibrated if full closure was not achieved. Post-operation, document the actual closure time, current readings, and any anomalies. If the MOV fails during operation, manual operation using the handwheel and gearbox may be required with proper understanding of gear ratios and required turns.
abnormal_variants: Position indicator shows 95% closed but flow continues

closure_technique.description: Controlled closure rate and pressure management

test_cases.scenario_description: An earthquake of magnitude 6.2 has occurred along the pipeline corridor. Multiple SCADA alarms are indicating pressure anomalies, valve position changes, and communication losses. Ground shaking was felt at multiple facilities. Gas leaks have been reported by the public in three locations.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Earthquake response requires activation of the Seismic Event Emergency Plan per 49 CFR 192.615. Immediate priorities are life safety, leak identification and isolation, and facility damage assessment. The control room must conduct a system status sweep to identify critical alarms - loss of communications may indicate damaged infrastructure. All field personnel should be accounted for and instructed to perform visual inspection of their assigned areas, reporting damage via radio. Public safety takes priority: reported leaks require immediate response teams dispatched to secure areas. Evacuation zones must be established at leak locations and ignition sources controlled. Earthquake damage can include pipeline rupture, bend or elbow stress, valve building cracks, and above-ground facility damage. SCADA anomalies may indicate actual damage or communication system failures - both require investigation. The system should be isolated at strategic points to control potential releases. Compressor stations should be shut down until damage assessment is complete. Natural gas odor reports should be treated as confirmed leaks until investigated. Underground leaks may be indicated by bubbling in standing water, vegetation kill, or ground settling - these require investigation with gas detection. Damaged facilities must be locked out until inspected by qualified engineers. Before restarting any system, all components must be visually inspected, leak surveyed, and pressure tested as required. Geotechnical assessment may be needed if ground movement or slope failures occurred. The event must be reported to DOT within required timeframes including the magnitude, affected mileage, and damage assessment. Post-earthquake, seismic monitoring should be evaluated for integration into the pipeline system.
abnormal_variants: Dam causes flooding in pipeline corridor

immediate_safety.description: Public evacuation and ignition source control

isolation.description: Strategic valve closures and station shutdowns

restart_requirements.description: Inspection and pressure testing before restart

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline pump station handling liquid petroleum products has experienced a seal failure on the mainline pump. Product is spraying and has ignited from an unknown source. The fire is partially contained within the pump bay area but spreading. The pump station has no automatic fire suppression system.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A petroleum fire at a pump station requires immediate life-safety and fire containment actions per 49 CFR 195.402 and the facility Emergency Action Plan. Immediate activation of the ESD is required to stop pumping, close suction and discharge valves, and isolate the station from the pipeline.
