Personnel must evacuate the building immediately using designated routes - no attempt should be made to fight the fire unless properly equipped and trained. The fire department must be notified immediately with information about the product type (petroleum), quantities, facility layout, and isolation valve locations. Station personnel should use portable fire extinguishers only if the fire is small and escape routes remain clear; otherwise evacuate and await professional response. If the fire is spreading, priority is protecting adjacent equipment and structures using available hose streams from a safe distance. The station should be depressurized if possible to reduce fuel supply to the fire. Firefighting water should be managed to prevent runoff contamination - containment berm integrity should be evaluated. No personnel should re-enter until cleared by fire officials. If the fire cannot be controlled, downwind evacuation of nearby areas may be required. After extinguishment, the area is contaminated and requires hazmat procedures before entry. Investigation must determine the ignition source and seal failure cause. DOT reporting is required per 49 CFR 195.54 if the fire causes damage, injury, or environmental release. The station must not restart until fire cause is determined and corrected, fire detection/suppression systems are upgraded, and management review is complete.
abnormal_variants: ESD fails to stop the pumps
Fire spreads to nearby tank farm

fire_suppression.description: ESD activation and fire department notification

containment.description: Fire spread prevention and runoff control

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline repair crew is working on a valve in a sour gas system when their personal H2S monitors begin alarming at 12 ppm. The repair is nearly complete with only final bolting remaining. The job is scheduled to take 4 more hours. Wind is calm making gas detection difficult.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An H2S alarm at 12 ppm during sour gas work requires immediate cessation and evacuation per API RP 55. Although the work is nearly complete, safety takes precedence. All personnel must immediately evacuate upwind to fresh air. The evacuation assembly point must be upwind at a minimum distance where readings are zero. Personnel must not remove respiratory protection until clear of the hazard. The alarm threshold at 12 ppm indicates the area is approaching the 15 ppm STEL - entry for any additional work requires supplied air respirators or SCBA. Given calm winds, the gas may be accumulating in low areas or the work area itself. Before re-entry, atmospheric monitoring with multiple monitors should map the gas cloud extent. The source must be identified - valve packing, flange gaskets, or residual trapped gas. If the source cannot be controlled by tightening bolts or minor adjustment, the work area may need additional ventilation using air movers or the repair postponed until conditions improve. The crew should not attempt to complete the work without proper PPE given the alarm. Wind conditions should be monitored and work resumed only when wind speed exceeds 5 mph to ensure dispersion. If work must continue, SCBA is required for all personnel in the area with a safety watch and backup air supply. The crew leader must assess whether completing this task is worth the increased exposure risk - if the valve is isolating a system, leaving it as-is may be safer than extended exposure. Documentation must include the alarm readings, evacuation actions, and decision to suspend or continue with enhanced PPE.
abnormal_variants: Gas cloud shifts and traps workers between leak and safe area
SCBA supply is only 30 minutes and work needs 4 hours
Wind shifts bringing gas toward assembly point

ppe_decision.description: SCBA requirement and work continuation decision

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline SCADA system shows a compressor station experiencing surge conditions on Unit 2. Suction pressure is oscillating rapidly and discharge temperature is rising above normal. Current flow is 80% of rated capacity.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Compressor surge is a serious condition that can cause severe damage to compressor internals per API 618 and manufacturer operating procedures. Immediate action is required to prevent catastrophic failure. The surge condition indicates the compressor is operating to the left of the surge line on the performance curve - flow has dropped too low for the given suction pressure and speed. Immediate response should include opening the recycle valve to increase flow through the compressor, moving operation back into the stable region. Anti-surge control systems should automatically activate; if not, manual recycle valve opening is required. The unit may need speed reduction to get back into operating envelope. If these measures fail, rapid isolation and shutdown may be necessary to prevent damage. Suction pressure oscillation can indicate check valve slamming or pulsation - pressure readings should be verified with local gauges to rule out instrument issues. Rising discharge temperature indicates reduced efficiency and potential internal rubbing. If temperatures exceed alarm limits, the unit must be shut down. After surge recovery, the unit must be inspected for damage including bearing condition, rotor balance, and seal integrity. Operating procedures must be reviewed to ensure adequate recycle flow margins are maintained. Anti-surge control settings should be verified and adjusted if necessary. If surge conditions recur, engineering review is needed to evaluate station piping and control valve sizing.

immediate_response.description: Recycle valve and surge control activation

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline company discovers that a farmer has been deep tilling within the pipeline right-of-way for several seasons without notification. The tilling depth is 24 inches and the pipe is typically at 36-inch depth. Recent aerial photos show the tilling patterns crossing the pipe alignment.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Agricultural activities in the right-of-way without notification constitute an encroachment requiring investigation per 49 CFR 192.614. Deep tilling to 24 inches with the pipe at 36 inches gives 12 inches of clearance. This may be within acceptable limits but requires verification. The operator must locate the exact pipe position and conduct depth of cover surveys to current conditions. Hand-digging is required to expose the pipe and assess whether tilling has damaged the coating, caused depressions, or created exposure due to soil settlement. Cathodic protection readings must be taken along the affected section to verify coating integrity. Any coating damage discovered requires repair per API 1160. If depth of cover has been reduced below minimums (typically 24-30 inches for agricultural areas), restoration is required. The farmer must be contacted to educate them on notification requirements for right-of-way activities. For recurring agricultural use, a right-of-way agreement or easement modification may be appropriate. If the farmer has been deliberately avoiding notification, legal enforcement may be necessary as deep tilling without locate can damage the pipe. Documentation must include GPS coordinates of the affected area, photos of the pipe condition, coating assessment reports, and correspondence with the landowner. If damage is found, the landowner may be billed for repair costs. If depth of cover is inadequate, soil must be imported and compacted to restore proper cover unless the risk assessment indicates monitoring is sufficient.

investigation.description: Pipe location, exposure, and damage assessment

test_cases.scenario_description: A school crossing guard reports hearing a hissing sound and detecting a gas odor near a high school crosswalk at dismissal time. The location is approximately 50 feet from the school building and 30 students are waiting at the crossing. The school is preparing for dismissal with 1200 students still inside.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A potential gas leak near a school at dismissal time is an imminent public safety emergency per 49 CFR 192.615. The combination of gas odor, audible hissing, and timing makes this a high-priority response. Immediate notifications must include the fire department, police, school principal, and dispatch. The school should be advised to shelter-in-place or evacuate based on gas readings - given the confirmed leak indicators, immediate evacuation is appropriate. The crossing area should be cordoned off and students redirected away from the hazard. Parents arriving for pickup must be diverted. Pipeline emergency responders must arrive quickly to assess the leak location using gas detection equipment. If readings exceed 20% LEL near the school, immediate building evacuation is mandatory. The school evacuation must be orderly with students and staff moving to upwind assembly points per the school emergency plan. Valves to isolate the leaking section must be located and closed. The leak source must be pinpointed and stopped or controlled. If the pipe is ruptured or severely damaged, the area must be expanded to full ERG Table 1-A evacuation distances. The school may need to close for the day and students transported to alternate locations. Coordination with school administration is critical to manage parent notification, student release, and media inquiries. After the leak is stopped, the area must be monitored until gas dissipates. The cause must be investigated and repairs made before service restoration. A root cause analysis must address why the leak developed in this high-consequence area.
abnormal_variants: Gas readings reach 40% LEL at school entrance
Parents arrive and attempt to access school during evacuation
Valve closure requires access through affected area

test_cases.scenario_description: A 20-inch natural gas pipeline at 580 psig requires hot tapping to install a temporary bypass flange for upcoming maintenance. The pipeline is API 5L X70 with 0.500-inch wall thickness. The hot tap must be performed during reduced flow conditions overnight to minimize customer impact.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping during reduced flow conditions requires additional planning per API 1104 and API 2201. Overnight operations must address lighting, fatigue management, and emergency response availability. The X70 high-strength material requires stricter controls on heat input and cooling rates. Preheat of 300°F is required for X70 at 0.500-inch thickness to prevent hydrogen cracking. Interpass temperature must not exceed 550°F to prevent detrimental microstructure changes. The hot tap must have a dedicated fire watch due to nighttime conditions - the fire watch must remain for minimum 30 minutes after welding completes. Lighting must meet electrical safety standards for Class I Division 1 areas - intrinsically safe or explosion-proof fixtures only. Backup lighting must be available for illumination failure. Personnel must be fully alert; rotating shifts or mandatory rest periods may be needed. The reduced flow condition means less cooling of the pipe during welding, which can be beneficial but requires careful monitoring of interpass temperature. Maximum allowable pressure during hot tapping must be calculated for X70 material at operating temperature. The temporary bypass hot tap must be designed for the full operating pressure even though it is temporary. The coupon must be retained for laboratory analysis of pipe material condition. All welds must be 100% inspected using ultrasonic testing after completion given the high-strength material. The temporary connection must be clearly marked and inspected daily during operation. Before removal, the pipe must be repaired following API 1104 procedures and the repair pressure tested. Documentation must include the overnight justification, material verification, and inspection records.

test_cases.scenario_description: A technician must enter a 48-inch pipeline during maintenance shutdown to inspect internal coating. The line is 8 miles long and has been isolated and depressurized. Initial atmospheric testing at the entry point shows O2 at 20.8%, LEL at 0%, H2S at 0 ppm, and CO2 at 15,000 ppm.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Entry into a pipeline with elevated CO2 requires special procedures per OSHA 1910.146 and API standards. At 15,000 ppm (1.5%), CO2 is well above the OSHA PEL of 5,000 ppm (8-hour TWE) and approaching the IDLH of 40,000 ppm. CO2 displaces oxygen and can cause unconsciousness, respiratory acidosis, and death at high concentrations. The pipeline atmosphere must be ventilated using forced air blowers before entry to reduce CO2 to below 5,000 ppm. Continuous monitoring during entry must include CO2 sensor as standard 4-gas monitors may not have CO2 capability - a dedicated CO2 monitor is required. Due to pipeline length (8 miles), remote entry locations should be considered to minimize travel in the pipe. Rescue planning is critical - standard confined space rescue may not be practicable in a long pipeline. A rescue team with specialized pipeline entry equipment including stretcher, retrieval systems, and extended reach capabilities must be on standby. Communication may require hard-wire systems or repeaters due to pipe length. The entry permit must address the specific CO2 hazard with modified alarm levels - 5,000 ppm warning, 10,000 ppm evacuation. PPE must include escape breathing apparatus (EEBA) for each entrant. Entry team should work in pairs, maintaining visual contact. The coating inspection should be prioritized to minimize entry time. If CO2 levels cannot be reduced below 5,000 ppm, supplied air respirators are required. Upon exit, entrants must be monitored for CO2 exposure symptoms including headache, dizziness, and shortness of breath.
abnormal_variants: CO2 rises to 30,000 ppm during entry

ppe.description: EEBA and supplied air as needed

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline company must excavate a 10-foot deep section over a 36-inch pipeline in active train right-of-way. The excavation is required for integrity inspection. Excavation can only occur during a 4-hour window when trains are not scheduled. The work area is between two active tracks.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Excavation in active railroad right-of-way requires specialized planning per OSHA 1926 Subpart P and Federal Railroad Administration (FRA) requirements. Track access permits are required from the railroad with specific time windows. Track protection must be established including flags, signals, or personnel as required by the railroad. Work between active tracks requires positive train control notification and potential watchmen/lookouts. If trains may pass during the window, workers must retreat to safe clearances - typically 15 feet from nearest rail. Trench protection is required at 10 feet depth - trench boxes or shoring must be selected for the soil conditions and track loading. Soil classification must consider surcharge loading from train traffic vibrations. A competent person must evaluate the excavation before each shift. Track ballast removal requires careful handling to prevent fouling the track bed - ballast must be separated and replaced to specification. Atmospheric testing is required before entry due to depth. Access/egress requires ladders or ramps within 25 feet. Spoil must be stored to maintain clearances from track - typically minimum 8 feet. Equipment must not foul track structure or overhead catenary if electrified. Train schedule coordination is critical - work must be capable of securing and clearing before next train. If work cannot be completed in the window, the excavation must be secured, backfilled, or bridged per railroad requirements. Damage to track or infrastructure must be reported immediately and repaired before train passage. The pipeline exposure must be completed, inspected, and backfilled within the window or rescheduled.
abnormal_variants: Train schedule delayed and work window shortened

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline operator needs to open a 24-inch block valve that has been closed for 15 years for a pipeline section reactivation. The valve is a gate valve with a manual gear operator. The pipeline was purged with nitrogen when shut down and the section is currently at atmospheric pressure.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Operating a valve closed for 15 years requires careful preparation per 49 CFR 192.745. Extended non-operation may result in valve seat seizure, packing hardening, and actuator gear issues. Before attempting operation, maintenance history should be reviewed and if unavailable, a conservative approach taken. The valve must be pre-lubricated if the valve design allows lubricant injection. The body drain must be opened to verify the valve cavity is not pressurized - trapped pressure can release violently. Packing gland bolts should be loosened slightly to reduce friction, then re-tightened after operation. The gear operator should be inspected for proper lubrication and gear engagement. Before operating, the operator should calculate required torque - frozen valves may require 2-3x normal torque. If the valve is seized, do not force operation; apply penetrating oil and wait, or schedule valve replacement. Initial operation should be in small increments (10-15% of full stroke) monitoring for binding or unusual resistance. If resistance is encountered, stop and reassess. The valve should be cycled multiple times if successful to distribute lubricant and verify operation. After opening, valve position must be verified locally and at SCADA. The valve should be stroked closed and reopened once to ensure full operability given its extended shutdown status. The packing should be checked for leakage during operation - 15-year-old packing may leak when disturbed. After operation, valve maintenance records must be updated with the operation date and any issues encountered. The valve should be added to regular exercise schedules to prevent future seizure.
abnormal_variants: Gate breaks when attempting to open
Body cavity contains significant trapped pressure
Packing leaks severely when valve moves

test_cases.scenario_description: A reported gas leak investigation discovers the leak is inside a nursing home mechanical room. Gas readings at the room entrance show 45% LEL. The nursing home has 60 residents, many non-ambulatory. Natural gas odor is strong.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A gas leak in a nursing home is a mass casualty event requiring coordinated emergency response per 49 CFR 192.615 and facility emergency plans. At 45% LEL in a mechanical room, immediate evacuation is critical. First responders must notify nursing home staff to initiate building evacuation using their internal procedures. Non-ambulatory residents require specialized evacuation including wheelchair transport, medical equipment management, and medication continuity. The gas source must be identified and isolated - typically a failed appliance, pipe joint, or valve. If the source is the building service line, the meter valve must be closed if accessible. If the source is internal building piping, the utility must close the main service valve. Fire department hazmat team should assist with gas monitoring to determine evacuation scope. Ignition sources must be eliminated - no electrical switching, cell phone use, or vehicle operation near the area. Nursing home evacuation to an alternate facility may be required - emergency management should coordinate reception facilities. Medical records and medication must accompany residents. Family notification must be coordinated through nursing home administration. The leak repair must be completed and the building thoroughly ventilated and gas tested before reoccupancy. Residents should only return after the building is certified safe by qualified personnel. The cause must be investigated and any systemic issues addressed. The DOT report may be required depending on leak volume and consequences. The nursing home may require fire marshal inspection before reopening.
abnormal_variants: Fire or explosion occurs during evacuation
Gas source cannot be immediately isolated

coordination.description: Nursing home staff and emergency management

test_cases.scenario_description: A welding spark has ignited insulation in the ceiling of a compressor building. The fire is above the dropped ceiling and not directly accessible. The compressor unit beneath is still running with suction pressure of 800 psig.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A concealed fire above a compressor with the unit running presents multiple hazards requiring immediate response per 49 CFR 192.615 and NFPA standards. The priority is personnel evacuation followed by compressor ESD. The fire alarm must be sounded immediately. The control room must be notified to initiate remote ESD of the compressor unit to stop fuel flow, close valves, and depressurize the unit. Personnel must not attempt to access the ceiling space while the compressor is running - the fire may have compromised gas lines, creating explosion risk. After ESD is confirmed, personnel with appropriate PPE may attempt fire suppression using available extinguishers through access panels if the fire is small. However, concealed fires often require fire department response with proper equipment. Fire department notification should include information about the concealed location, gas hazards, and ongoing depressurization. The area should be evacuated to at least 200 feet given the high pressure gas source below the fire. If fire spreads or ESD fails, full evacuation is required. Station deluge or sprinkler systems should be activated if they can reach the concealed space. Firefighters accessing the ceiling must be informed of the gas hazards and potential for compromised lines. After extinguishment, the ceiling space must be inspected for remaining fire, gas line damage, and structural integrity. The compressor must not be restarted until full inspection, fire cause investigation, and management approval. Smoke and water damage to electrical and control equipment must be assessed.
calculation_requirements: Fire growth rate in concealed space
abnormal_variants: Fire breaches gas line in ceiling
Firefighters arrive but unaware of gas hazard

concealed_space.description: Concealed fire tactics and gas hazard communication

test_cases.scenario_description: A sour gas dehydration unit is being started up after maintenance. During startup, gas breakthrough occurs at the regenerator vent containing 5% H2S at 200°F. The vent releases at 30 feet elevation. Downwind readings show 25 ppm at ground level.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: H2S release during startup of sour gas equipment requires immediate response per API RP 55 and facility emergency procedures. At 25 ppm ground level, the area exceeds safe working limits and requires evacuation. Personnel must immediately evacuate upwind to the assembly point. The unit startup must be suspended - attempting to continue through the release may increase exposure. The regenerator vent must be routed to the incinerator or flare if the system design allows - direct atmospheric venting of high-H2S gas indicates process upset or improper startup sequence. If the vent cannot be diverted, the unit must be shut in at the inlet until the release can be controlled. Downwind areas must be monitored - if H2S approaches occupied areas, public warning and evacuation may be required. Emergency shutdown of the unit should include closing inlet and outlet valves and depressuring to a closed system. Personnel re-entry to address the release requires SCBA and full Level A or Level B PPE. The vent gas temperature at 200°F indicates the regenerator is still hot - thermal expansion may be contributing to venting. The startup procedure must be reviewed - H2S breakthrough indicates inadequate dehydration bed regeneration or flow sequencing error. After the release is stopped, the area must be monitored until H2S is below 10 ppm. Medical surveillance of exposed personnel is required per OSHA. The incident requires investigation before restart - startup procedures must be reviewed and potentially modified to prevent recurrence.
abnormal_variants: H2S reaches 100 ppm at facility boundary
Incinerator pilot is out and cannot relight
Unit cannot be shut in due to pressure

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline SCADA system indicates an electrical fault on the cathodic protection rectifier for a river crossing section. The pipeline potential has shifted from -0.85V to -0.45V. The crossing is 800 feet long under a navigable waterway with commercial barge traffic.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Loss of cathodic protection on a river crossing is a serious integrity concern requiring immediate response per 49 CFR 192.465 and 192.921. The potential shift from -0.85V to -0.45V indicates active corrosion conditions are likely occurring. The rectifier fault must be diagnosed immediately - common causes include AC power loss, blown fuses, cable damage, or rectifier failure. If AC power is available, rectifier troubleshooting should attempt to restore operation. If the rectifier cannot be repaired quickly, temporary protection may be needed including temporary anodes or bonding. However, permanent repair must address the root cause. Given this is a river crossing, the high-consequence area (HCA) status means integrity threats require urgent attention. If protection cannot be restored within acceptable timeframes (typically 60 days per some operators, sooner for HCAs), inspection or repair may be required. The crossing should be evaluated for potential external corrosion damage using inline inspection or direct assessment. If CP cannot be maintained and the line is in an HCA, pressure reduction or service interruption pending repair may be necessary per 49 CFR 192.935 and 192.937. The Coast Guard must be notified if the situation affects navigation - cathodic protection anodes are sometimes in navigable channels. Before any underwater work, permits and notifications are required. Post-repair, the CP system must be tested to verify -0.85V or more negative potential is restored. Electrical isolation testing should verify no shorts have developed. The incident must be documented per integrity management requirements including evaluation of repair vs replace decisions.
abnormal_variants: Rectifier shows physical damage from boat anchor
CP pipe coating damage discovered at crossing
Rectifier cannot be repaired for 30 days

test_cases.scenario_description: A contractor performing directional drilling for fiber optic installation contacts the pipeline One Call center. The proposed bore path parallels a 30-inch transmission pipeline at 25-foot lateral distance for 2 miles. The bore depth will be 8 feet and the pipeline is at 48 inches depth.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A directional drilling parallel to a transmission pipeline requires careful damage prevention procedures per 49 CFR 192.614 and PHMSA Advisory Bulletins. Paralleling for 2 miles presents significant risk if the bore path deviates. The operator must evaluate the proposed path and establish requirements. Hand-dug verification potholes are required to confirm pipe location and depth at intervals - typically every 500-1000 feet for parallel installations. The bore path must be tracked using electronic methods with deviation monitoring - typically electromagnetic or gyroscopic tracking. Maximum allowable deviation must be specified - typically 5-10 feet depending on soil and risk. If deviation exceeds limits, drilling must stop and the path re-established. The pipeline operator should require an on-site observer during drilling operations. The boring fluid pressure must be monitored and limited - excessive pressure can cause frac-outs or ground heave that stresses the pipe. If frac-outs occur, drilling must stop and the impact on the pipeline assessed. The gap between bore and pipe is only 4 feet (8 feet minus 4 feet pipe depth), which is minimal. Additional separation or depth may be required. The pipeline operator may require the contractor to shift the bore path farther from the pipe or change to open-cut installation in the parallel section. Post-installation, the pipe must be exposed at selected locations to verify no damage occurred and depth of cover remains adequate. Cathodic protection must be checked as direct current from drilling equipment can affect the CP system. The crossing must be added to patrol routes and monitored for settlement during the first year.
abnormal_variants: Bore path deviates 15 feet toward pipeline
Drilling fluid frac-out occurs between bore and pipe

test_cases.scenario_description: An anonymous threat has been called in claiming explosive devices have been placed at two valve stations. The threat mentions specific valve numbers and includes details about the pipeline. The threat states devices will detonate in 3 hours.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A bomb threat against pipeline facilities is a security emergency requiring immediate action per 49 CFR 192.615 and the company security plan. First priority is personnel safety - all personnel must evacuate the threatened valve stations immediately. Control room must verify valve numbers match actual facilities and identify the affected pipeline sections. Law enforcement must be notified immediately - typically FBI, local police, and potentially homeland security given critical infrastructure status. The affected valve stations must be secured - no approach within minimum standoff distances (typically 300 feet) pending bomb squad clearance. SCADA functions can be used to operate valves remotely if needed for system safety. If valves need to be closed to isolate the threatened sections, remote operation should be used rather than sending personnel. Customer impacts from isolating sections must be evaluated and notifications made. Emergency management and public safety officials must be briefed on pipeline hazards including isolation and blowdown capabilities if needed. If the threat is credible and the timeline believed, consideration may be given to controlled depressurization of affected sections to reduce explosion consequences. Media inquiries will need coordination to prevent panic while maintaining public information. After law enforcement clears the facilities, normal operations may resume. The incident must be reported to DOT and Transportation Security Administration (TSA) Pipeline Security Division. Security measures at other facilities should be increased until the threat is resolved. Post-incident, security protocols should be reviewed.
abnormal_variants: Bomb squad finds device at first location

operational_response.description: Remote valve operation and system isolation

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline requires hot tapping to install a monitoring thermowell on a 10-inch steam trace line wrapped around a 36-inch crude oil pipeline. The trace line operates at 250 psig and 450°F. The main crude line operates at 600 psig.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping a steam trace line adjacent to a high-pressure crude pipeline presents compound hazards requiring specialized procedures per API 1104. Steam service at 450°F requires high-temperature materials and procedures. The thermowell installation must account for thermal expansion and contraction cycling. The steam line must be isolated and drained before hot tapping to prevent steam release and water hammer. Even though the line is steam, isolation is critical as residual pressure can cause severe burns. The hot tap equipment must be rated for high temperature service including gaskets and seals compatible with 450°F. The welding on the steam line must not damage the adjacent crude pipeline - heat shielding or wet wrap may be needed to protect the main line coating. Preheat for the steam line may already be satisfied given the operating temperature, but cooling during the work must be managed. The steam line material must be verified - typically carbon steel for this service. After the tap is complete, the steam system must be slowly repressurized to prevent thermal shock to the thermowell weld. The entire assembly must be pressure tested at steam working pressure before returning to service. The crude pipeline should be monitored for temperature rise from the welding heat input - if the crude temperature increases significantly, cool-down measures may be needed. Documentation must include the steam service classification, material verification, and thermal protection measures for the main line.
abnormal_variants: Steam line not fully isolated - residual pressure released
Welding heat damages crude pipeline coating
Thermowell weld fails during steam restart

test_cases.scenario_description: A technician must enter a scrubber vessel to replace internal packing. The vessel is 8 feet diameter and 20 feet tall with entry through a 24-inch manway at the top. The vessel contained amine solution and has been drained and purged with nitrogen for 24 hours. Initial readings show O2 at 19.2%, LEL at 0%, and CO2 at 8,000 ppm.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Entry into an amine scrubber requires careful confined space procedures per OSHA 1910.146 and API standards. At 19.2% O2 and 8,000 ppm CO2, the space is potentially hazardous. CO2 levels must be reduced below 5,000 ppm before entry. The nitrogen purge must be vented and fresh air introduced using forced ventilation. The vessel must be fully isolated using blinds or blinds-list in addition to valves - amine systems can have significant residual liquid. Internal residue of amine solution can release CO2 and other gases when disturbed - the packing removal may increase off-gassing. Continuous four-gas monitoring is required including CO2 monitoring which may require a fifth sensor. Entry requires full-body harness with retrieval line and tripod/winch system at the manway. Ventilation must be maintained throughout entry - blower discharge should be to safe area as amine vapors are toxic. Amine exposure symptoms include respiratory irritation, skin burns, and eye damage - appropriate chemical PPE is required including face shield and chemical-resistant gloves. The work should be planned to minimize entry time. A standby rescue team is required given the difficult access of a vertical entry. Rescue planning must consider the chemical hazards - rescue personnel need SCBA and chemical protection. Communication must be maintained between entrant and attendant. If gas readings change during entry, evacuation is required. The removed packing will be contaminated with amine and requires hazardous waste disposal per EPA regulations. Post-entry, the space should be re-purged if return to service will be delayed.
abnormal_variants: CO2 spikes to 15,000 ppm during packing removal
Entrant begins showing amine exposure symptoms

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline integrity dig requires excavation next to a 16-inch high-pressure gas line in an area with undocumented mine shafts from the early 1900s. Historical maps show abandoned mine workings in the general area. The excavation must be 12 feet deep.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Excavation near undocumented mine workings presents significant geotechnical hazards requiring special procedures. A thorough historical investigation must be conducted including review of state mining archives, USGS databases, and local historical societies. Geophysical testing including ground-penetrating radar (GPR) or electrical resistivity should be conducted to identify voids or disturbed earth. The excavation should begin with test pits using equipment capable of identifying voids before full-size excavation proceeds. A qualified engineer or engineering geologist must evaluate the site. The excavation protective system must account for potential voids - standard shoring may be inadequate if a mine shaft is encountered. If a void is discovered, work must stop immediately and the void evaluated - it may contain hazardous atmosphere, water, or be structurally unstable. Entry into discovered mine workings is prohibited without proper mine rescue preparation including mine gases testing, ventilation, and specialized rescue equipment. The pipeline excavation should be relocated if mine hazards are identified. If relocation is not possible, the void must be professionally filled or bridged before work proceeds. OSHA 1926 Subpart P requires any excavation with unusual conditions including voids to have an engineered protective system. Daily inspections by a competent person must include watching for signs of underground voids including soil sloughing, unusual sounds, or tool drops indicating depth variations. If a mine shaft opening is discovered, it must be secured to prevent inadvertent entry and reported to state mining authorities. The excavation approach must use methods that can detect voids before personnel are exposed.
abnormal_variants: Mine shaft discovered at excavation depth

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline requires valve operations to redirect flow around a section containing a developing leak. The plan requires closing three valves and opening two crossover valves to establish a bypass configuration. The system currently carries 800 MMSCFD at 1000 psig.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Redirecting flow around a leaking section requires careful valve sequencing per 49 CFR 192.615 and 192.745. The operation must be coordinated with the control center to manage pressure transients and flow redirection. Before operations, the bypass section must be verified as clear and rated for full operating pressure. The sequence should minimize pressure spikes and ensure continuous flow around the leak. Typical sequence: first open the downstream crossover valve to establish the bypass path; then close the valves to isolate the leak section working from downstream to upstream; finally close the upstream crossover valve to complete the bypass. However, each system is different and must follow the specific valve lineup procedure. Pressure must be monitored throughout - readings before and after each valve operation should show expected changes. A significant unexpected pressure change indicates valve malfunction or line condition. Flow must be maintained to customers - if the bypass cannot handle full flow, load shedding may be required. Valve operators must understand the system hydraulics to avoid creating dead legs or pressure traps. After the bypass is established, the isolated section can be depressurized for repair. All valve positions must be verified locally and at SCADA. If any valve fails to operate properly, the sequence must be stopped and evaluated. The bypass may have different surge characteristics - the control center must update their models. Documentation must include valve positions before/after, pressures, flow rates, and timing of each operation.
abnormal_variants: Bypass valve will not open fully

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline patrol discovers a backhoe has struck and ruptured a 4-inch steel gas service line feeding a commercial development. Gas is venting at high pressure creating a visible vapor cloud. The backhoe operator is unconscious in the cab. The wind is blowing the vapor toward an occupied office building 150 feet away.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A ruptured high-pressure gas line with an injured operator is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate response per 49 CFR 192.615. First responders must prioritize scene safety - the downwind office building must be evacuated immediately given the wind direction and distance. The rupture area must be secured to exclude vehicles and pedestrians. Rescuers must not approach the backhoe or attempt extraction without respiratory protection - SCBA is required to enter the vapor cloud. The operator extraction is challenging because the cab is in the hot zone; rescue must wait until the gas is controlled or proper PPE is available. Simultaneously, the gas service must be isolated by locating and closing the nearest upstream valve - typically at the main or service tee. Fire department must be notified for rescue standby and to control ignition sources in the area. Once the upstream valve is closed, residual gas can vent until the line is depressurized. After isolation, the vapor cloud will dissipate and rescue can proceed. The injured operator requires immediate medical attention once extracted - gas exposure, potential trauma from the incident, and other injuries must be assessed. The ruptured pipe must be repaired - typically by cutting out the damaged section and installing a new service pipe. Before restoration, pressure testing is required. The backhoe operator's employer must be identified for investigation of One Call compliance per 49 CFR 192.614. If the strike was due to lack of notification, enforcement action may be pursued.
abnormal_variants: Upstream valve is frozen and cannot be closed

test_cases.scenario_description: A fire has occurred in the electrical substation supplying a compressor station. The transformer oil ignited from an arc flash and the fire is spreading to nearby cable trays. The compressor station is running on backup power but may lose all power if the fire continues.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An electrical substation fire at a compressor station requires immediate multi-hazard response per NFPA 70E and facility emergency action plans. Electrical fires cannot be fought with water or standard extinguishers - Class C rated extinguishers or CO2 may be used if power is confirmed off. If power cannot be de-energized immediately, only qualified electrical personnel with proper PPE may attempt limited suppression. The first priority is notifying the electric utility to de-energize the substation. Compressor station personnel should not attempt to fight energized electrical fires - evacuate and await fire department with electrical safety training. The control room must prepare for total power loss - identify critical systems needing backup power or safe shutdown. The compressor units may need to be shut down before power is lost to prevent uncontrolled events. If fire spreads to cable trays, large sections of the station may lose power and communication. After electrical isolation, standard firefighting can proceed. Water and foam can be used on the transformer oil once de-energized. Environmental containment is needed for oil runoff - the station should have oil containment systems. After extinguishment, electrical testing must confirm de-energization before investigation. Spare transformer and cable inventory should be assessed for restoration. The cause must be investigated including transformer maintenance records, load analysis, and protection coordination. Restart requires electrical testing, management approval, and system commissioning. DOT reporting may be required if fire affects pipeline operations or causes damage.
abnormal_variants: Fire causes total station blackout before controlled shutdown
Utility cannot quickly de-energize the substation

station_shutdown.description: Controlled compressor shutdown before power loss

test_cases.scenario_description: A sour gas well is being brought online after workover. During startup, the wellhead choke fails and releases high-velocity gas containing 8% H2S. The wind is calm. Gas is accumulating in the low areas around the well pad where 6 workers are performing tasks.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A high-volume H2S release at 8% (80,000 ppm) with calm wind creates immediate IDLH conditions requiring emergency response per API RP 55. At these concentrations, a single breath can cause immediate unconsciousness and death. Workers must evacuate immediately using available escape breathing apparatus or holding breath until clear. The general alarm must be activated. The well must be shut in using the surface safety valve (SSV) or subsurface safety valve if accessible - however, approaching the wellhead is extremely hazardous given the H2S concentration. If remote shutdown is not available, the release may continue until well pressure depletes. All personnel must evacuate upwind and to higher ground as H2S is heavier than air. The calm wind creates the worst-case scenario of accumulation - evacuation distances must be expanded significantly (potentially 1+ mile). Emergency services must be notified with specific H2S information - first responders arriving without SCBA will be unable to approach. The well pad and downwind areas must be secured from all entry. Medical facilities must be alerted for potential casualties. Continuous air monitoring must track the gas cloud extent. If workers are overcome, rescue should not be attempted without SCBA - this is a fundamental safety principle as would-be rescuers commonly become additional victims in H2S incidents. After the release stops, entry requires full Level A suits with SCBA. The well must be repaired and tested before restart. The incident must be reported to multiple agencies including OSHA, EPA, and state oil and gas regulators. Response procedures should be reviewed to ensure adequate remote shutdown capability.
abnormal_variants: Rescue team arrives without SCBA and attempts entry

immediate_evacuation.description: Alarm and evacuation to high ground

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline is experiencing unexplained pressure variations of 20-30 psig occurring every 15 minutes between two compressor stations. The pattern suggests pulsation or surge in the pipeline segment. The line operates at 900 psig and the variations coincide with one compressor unit cycling.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Unexplained pressure pulsations in transmission pipelines can indicate compressor surge acoustic waves, valve instability, or other serious conditions per 49 CFR 192.617. The 20-30 psi variation at 15-minute intervals suggests a frequency that could cause fatigue damage to pipe, valves, or equipment. The pattern coinciding with compressor cycling indicates the source - likely acoustic resonance from the compressor affecting the pipeline. Immediate investigation must determine if the source is the compressor or a valve issue. Compressor surge creates pressure waves that travel through the system - anti-surge control adjustment may be needed. If the pulsation is from acoustic resonance, operating conditions or piping configuration may need adjustment. The pipeline should be monitored for vibration at locations along the affected section - excessive vibration can cause fatigue failures in pipe, supports, or appurtenances. Pressure monitoring should record the pulsation waveform for analysis. If the pulsations cannot be eliminated by operational adjustment, engineering review is needed. Temporary measures may include reducing flow or adjusting compressor speed. The affected pipeline section should be inspected for fatigue indications including weld cracks and support damage. SCADA should be reviewed to identify when the pattern started - recent maintenance, flow changes, or equipment changes may be the cause. If the pressure variations represent a safety threat (exceeding design fatigue limits), pressure reduction or service interruption may be required pending resolution.

test_cases.scenario_description: A contractor has completed bore under a pipeline using horizontal directional drilling. Post-drill excavation reveals the bore path passed 3 feet below the pipe (within the pipe foundation) and the bore intersected the pipeline's cathodic protection anode bed, destroying 30% of the anodes.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An HDD intersecting the pipeline foundation and anode bed is a reportable incident requiring immediate response per 49 CFR 192.614 and 192.705. The proximity of 3 feet below the pipe is within the pipe zone and creates settlement risk. The excavation must be expanded to expose the entire affected area. The pipe must be inspected for coating damage, gouges, or deformation from drilling fluids or settlement. A cathodic protection survey must be conducted including pipe-to-soil potentials along the affected section. The loss of 30% of anodes significantly degrades protection levels - rectifier output will need adjustment and replacement anodes required. The drilling contractor has caused damage and costs - documentation must support claims for repair and remediation. The excavation should reveal the actual bore path - if deviation records show the path was within tolerance, the bore plan may have been inadequate. The pipe must be evaluated for structural integrity - drilling under pipe creates void spaces that can lead to settlement and stress. The foundation disturbance requires engineering evaluation of support and potential grouting. The anode bed must be replaced - engineering design must account for the disturbed soil conditions. The bore pit must be monitored for settlement during and after backfill. If coating damage is found, immediate repair per NACE requirements is necessary. The incident must be reported to DOT if the damage meets reportable thresholds. The contractor's insurance and remediation responsibility must be established. Future crossings in the area must include increased monitoring and possibly different construction methods.

repair_requirements.description: Anode bed replacement and coating repair

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline company has been notified that high-pressure gas service is being installed to a new residential development. The development is being constructed directly over an existing 20-inch transmission pipeline easement. Construction is 75% complete with houses occupied.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Discovery of residential construction over an existing transmission pipeline requiring assessment per 49 CFR 192.614 and high consequence area (HCA) considerations. The encroachment of residential structures within the original easement is a serious integrity concern. The operator must dispatch personnel to assess what has been built over the pipeline and document violations. The pipeline may need reclassification as an HCA due to the residential development. Integrity management requirements may now include additional inspections, pressure testing, or remediation. The construction over the easement violates typical easement terms allowing only agricultural use. Legal action may be needed to address the encroachment. If structures are within the prescribed easement width, the developer and homeowners may be required to remove or relocate structures, or the pipeline may require relocation at developer expense. Safety assessments must evaluate whether the occupied structures present an unacceptable risk that requires immediate action. If the pipeline integrity is threatened, emergency orders may be needed to stop occupancy or require protective measures. Integrity assessment including inline inspection or hydrostatic testing may be required before accepting the changed conditions. The pipeline operator must notify affected customers of the presence of the transmission line and provide emergency contact information. Future maintenance access must be evaluated - structures blocking access may prevent inspection and repair. This situation requires coordination between operations, legal, land management, and regulatory compliance. PHMSA notification may be required for HCA reclassification and integrity plan changes.
abnormal_variants: Occupied homes are within 25 feet of pipe centerline
Pipeline integrity shows current anomalies in the section

hca_reclassification.description: IM program updates and integrity assessment

test_cases.scenario_description: A 24-inch pipeline requires installation of a corrosion monitoring coupon via hot tap. The coupon will be used to evaluate internal corrosion rates. The pipeline operates at 450 psig and 120°F. The pipe is API 5L X42 with 0.312-inch wall thickness showing external corrosion pitting in the area.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping a pipeline with existing external corrosion requires careful evaluation per API 1104 and NACE standards. The external pitting must be assessed before hot tapping to ensure adequate remaining wall thickness. Ultrasonic thickness testing (UT) must be performed at multiple points around the circumference to map the corrosion. The minimum acceptable wall for hot tapping must be verified - typically 0.250-inch for a 24-inch line at this pressure, but specific company requirements may apply. If wall thickness is marginal, alternative locations should be selected. The hot tap location should avoid areas of deepest pitting. The welding heat input must be controlled to prevent burn-through of thinned areas - heat input should be minimized and travel speed maintained. Preheat requirements must consider the X42 material grade - typically 150-200°F for this thickness. The tapping machine must be set up carefully to avoid gouging thinned areas during drilling. After completion, the area must be evaluated for CP interference - the hot tap connection may affect cathodic protection current distribution. Coating repair around the hot tap must follow NACE standards. If the corrosion indicates active external corrosion, additional CP assessment may be needed. The corrosion coupon installation location should provide representative exposure - avoid installing directly over pitting. Post-installation, the coupon must be connected to a test station for monitoring. The hot tap coupon connection includes an isolating union - electrical isolation must be verified with a resistance check. The incident highlights the need for integrity review of external corrosion in the area.
abnormal_variants: UT reveals wall thickness below minimum
Coupon location coincides with active corrosion cell

technique.description: Low heat input welding for thinned pipe

location_selection.description: Avoiding pitting and selecting representative coupon location

test_cases.scenario_description: A contractor is performing abrasive blasting inside a 10-foot diameter storage tank that previously contained hydrocarbon liquids. The tank has been cleaned and certified gas-free. The work involves blasting the internal surfaces for recoating.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Abrasive blasting in a storage tank is permit-required confined space entry with additional hazards from the blasting operation per OSHA 1910.146 and OSHA 1926.55. The tank must be fully ventilated with forced air at sufficient rates to remove airborne particulates - typically 20 air changes per hour minimum, higher for blasting operations. Continuous atmospheric monitoring is required for oxygen, combustible gas, and respirable particulates - silica or other abrasive materials create additional exposure risk. The blast operator must wear appropriate respiratory protection - typically supplied air blasting hoods for abrasive blasting, not cartridge respirators. The attendant must watch for signs of dust accumulation affecting visibility and communication. Non-sparking tools and intrinsically safe equipment are required. The blasting operation creates static electricity - proper grounding of the blasting equipment and tank is essential. Fire watch must be maintained during blasting as sparks from the operation can ignite residual hydrocarbons even if the tank was gas-freed. No other hot work should be conducted simultaneously. The blaster must be trained in confined space entry in addition to blasting certification. A safety harness with retrieval line may be required depending on tank configuration - if the floor is sloped or has obstacles. Lighting must be adequate for safe blasting - explosion-proof fixtures rated for the classified area. After blasting, the dust must be allowed to settle and be removed before air quality testing confirms safe entry for other workers. The abrasive material may be hazardous waste if contaminated with hydrocarbons - disposal must follow EPA regulations. Medical surveillance including chest X-rays is required for workers repeatedly exposed to silica dust.

ignition_control.description: Grounding, fire watch, and hot work

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline operator must excavate a buried valve vault for inspection. The vault is 6 feet deep with a history of water accumulation. The vault roof is precast concrete panels. This is the first excavation of this vault in 10 years.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Excavating a long-unaccessed vault requires planning per OSHA 1926 Subpart P and confined space procedures. The soil classification around the vault must be determined before excavation begins. The precast panels may not be designed for surcharge loading - spoil must be kept well clear of the panels to prevent overload. Groundwater accumulation suggests poor drainage that must be addressed during the excavation. Dewatering may be required before safe entry - pumps must be placed outside the excavation. Entry into the vault is confined space - atmospheric testing and permit procedures apply. The vault atmosphere should be tested for oxygen, combustible gas, hydrogen sulfide (common in vaults), and carbon monoxide. Ventilation must be established before entry. The vault interior must be inspected for structural integrity - concrete spalling, corrosion of ladder rungs, and coating condition. Access/egress requires a secured ladder meeting OSHA requirements - existing ladders may be degraded. The vault interior lighting must be explosion-proof if it is a gas vault. All equipment lowered into the vault must be rated for the atmosphere. If the vault shows structural compromise, work may need to stop and engineering consulted. The vault inspection should include valve condition, position indicator functionality, bypass piping, and drainage. After inspection, the vault must be properly cleaned and closed. Documentation should update the vault as-built and inspection records. The next inspection interval should be established based on condition. Any deficiencies discovered must be scheduled for repair.
abnormal_variants: Structural crack discovered in vault wall

confined_space.description: Atmospheric testing and vault entry procedures

test_cases.scenario_description: An operator is closing a 36-inch gate valve with a motor operator when the torque switch trips, halting closure at 80% complete. The valve shows 15% seat leakage at this position. The control room indicates the valve is needed for upcoming system isolation.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A gate valve stopping at 80% closure with torque switch trip requires investigation per 49 CFR 192.745. The torque switch is designed to prevent over-torquing the valve - it may indicate increased resistance in the valve mechanism or may be set too low. The 15% seat leakage at partial closure is expected for a gate valve - full closure should reduce leakage. The valve should not be forced past the torque limit. The operator should verify valve position locally - valve position indicators on motor operators can disagree with actual position. If the valve is actually 80% closed, attempting reset and continuing closure may be possible. However, the cause of high torque must be understood - possible causes include: debris in the valve, damaged seats, bent stem, or gear issues. The valve should be examined for external signs of damage. The motor operator should be checked for proper operation including limit switch and torque switch settings. If the valve must operate for upcoming isolation, and time permits, maintenance should be scheduled before the critical operation. If the valve must operate now, a controlled attempt to complete closure with close monitoring may be justified, with a plan to stop if torque continues high. After the time-critical operation passes, the valve must be disassembled and inspected. The torque switch setting should be verified against manufacturer specifications. If the valve is essential for system operation, a backup isolation plan should be identified in case the valve fails completely during operation.
abnormal_variants: Valve will not reopen after partial closure
SCADA shows position but local shows different

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline company receives multiple odor complaints across a 5-mile section of distribution main. Complaints are coming from residences, businesses, and a school. The distribution system operates at 60 psig with multiple feeds. Weather is overcast with temperature inversion conditions.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Multiple odor complaints over a wide area suggests a significant leak or system-wide issue per 49 CFR 192.615 and 192.617. Temperature inversion conditions prevent normal dispersion, keeping gas near ground level and allowing it to travel farther. Immediate actions include dispatching multiple crews to investigate complaints, begin leak survey, and notify fire departments in the affected areas. Odor complaints must be treated as confirmed leaks until investigated. Priority locations include the school, which may require evacuation if gas is detected nearby. The distribution system should be evaluated for common mode failures - regulator station issues, pipeline damage affecting multiple branches, or odorant injection problems. If odorant is not being added properly, the entire system may lack odor despite containing gas. Crews should carry gas detectors and check complaint locations, low areas, storm drains, and manholes where gas may accumulate. If multiple leaks are found, the affected section may need isolation. System pressure should be monitored - a pressure drop indicates significant leakage. If the cause cannot be quickly identified, consideration should be given to isolating sections and reducing system pressure while investigating. Media coordination may be needed if the area is large. If odorant injection is the cause, the system must be evaluated for unodorized gas pockets and re-odorized. Documentation must track all complaint locations, investigation results, and actions taken. DOT reporting may apply if significant gas is released or service interrupted.

test_cases.scenario_description: A fire has occurred in the battery room of a pipeline valve station. The valve station has lead-acid battery banks for SCADA backup power. Smoke is coming from the battery ventilation system. The station has a deluge system but no specific battery fire suppression.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A battery room fire requires specialized response per NFPA 850 and NFPA 111. Lead-acid batteries produce hydrogen gas during charging which is explosive. The smoke may be from burning plastic cases and insulation combined with hydrogen gas ignition. Immediate action is to disconnect the battery charger using remote controls - do not enter the room to disconnect manually. The control room should be notified and station ESD evaluated - however, battery room fires may not constitute immediate pipeline isolation if the hazard is contained. Personnel must evacuate the station if smoke is spreading. Fire department notification should emphasize the battery chemistry and hydrogen hazards. Standard fire extinguishers are appropriate for battery fires - Class A for combustibles, Class C if electrical sources not isolated. Water or foam can be used once power is confirmed off. The room ventilation should remain running if safe to do so to ventilate hydrogen. Firefighters approaching the room should be warned about acid exposure from damaged batteries - full PPE including acid-resistant gear and respiratory protection. After extinguishment, the room must be ventilated before entry. Damaged batteries require hazardous waste handling per EPA regulations - spilled acid and damaged lead plates are regulated waste. The cause must be investigated - battery room fires often result from overcharging, short circuits, or poor ventilation. The battery system should be inspected and tested before restoring backup power. If SCADA backup is compromised, the station may need to be staffed until power is restored.
abnormal_variants: Battery acid spreads to electrical panels
SCADA loses backup power during incident

electrical_safety.description: Remote charger disconnect, no manual entry

test_cases.scenario_description: A crew is conducting pigging operations on a sour gas pipeline. During pig receiver opening, residual gas escapes with H2S level of 50 ppm detected at the receiver door. The pig is partially stuck and requires manual extraction.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An H2S release at 50 ppm during pig receiver operations requires immediate cessation and respiratory protection per API RP 55. At 50 ppm, IDLH conditions are approached - extended exposure causes serious health effects including eye irritation, respiratory distress, and potential pulmonary edema. Personnel must immediately evacuate upwind to the assembly point where readings are below 10 ppm. The receiver door should be partially reclosed if safe to do so to reduce emissions, but not locked. Fresh air ventilation should be directed at the receiver if equipment is available. No personnel should approach the receiver without SCBA or full-face respirator with H2S cartridges and escape provisions. The stuck pig requires careful extraction using tools operated from outside the high-concentration zone - extension tools or remote handling equipment if available. If manual extraction is required, it must be done with SCBA and a safety watch in communication. The receiver should be purged before full opening using nitrogen or air blowing if the system allows. The pig receiver design may have a vent valve that can be opened to release residual gas to a safe location. The pig itself may have accumulated liquids that release H2S when disturbed - extraction may need to proceed slowly with pauses for gas dispersion. All personnel must be trained in H2S response and have undergone medical surveillance. If readings remain high, operations should be suspended until better conditions or enhanced PPE is available. The receiver procedures should be reviewed to prevent future incidents.
abnormal_variants: H2S increases to 100 ppm when pig moves
Safety watch also exposed during rescue attempt

immediate_evacuation.description: Clear area upwind to safe levels

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline SCADA system shows erratic pressure readings at a meter station with readings jumping between 700 and 950 psig rapidly. Other stations show stable conditions. The meter station has a turbine meter and differential pressure transmitters.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Erratic pressure readings suggesting instrument malfunction versus actual pressure events per 49 CFR 192.617. The readings jumping by 250 psi rapidly at one station while others are stable strongly suggests instrument issues rather than actual pipeline conditions. First steps include verifying the readings with local gauges - if local gauges show stable pressure, the issue is instrumentation. Common causes include: loose wiring connections, failed transmitter, freezing of impulse lines, or electrical interference. Impulse lines on differential pressure transmitters can freeze in cold weather causing erratic readings. Communication with the meter station should verify ambient conditions. If impulse lines are suspect, heat tracing may be needed. The wiring connections to the transmitter should be checked for tightness. The transmitter may need calibration or replacement. If actual pressure instability is confirmed by local gauges, the cause may be a check valve chattering, regulator instability, or upstream equipment. Check for associated flow variations which would indicate actual process changes. The control room should isolate the erratic signal from control logic to prevent automatic responses based on false readings. Data historians should be reviewed to identify when the pattern started - correlating with weather events, maintenance, or equipment changes. Field technicians should carry spare transmitters if replacement is indicated. If the issue cannot be resolved quickly and reliable pressure indication is needed for safe operation, the station may need to operate with manual monitoring or reduced flow until repaired.
abnormal_variants: Control system responds to false signal

isolation.description: Remove from control logic if false

test_cases.scenario_description: A contractor is blasting rock for a utility trench 25 feet from a high-pressure gas transmission pipeline. The blasting contractor has valid permits and is following standard procedures. No pipeline personnel are on site.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Rock blasting near transmission pipelines requires specific damage prevention procedures per 49 CFR 192.614 and 192.605. Blasting creates ground vibration and air overpressure that can damage pipeline coating, welds, and supports. The pipeline operator should have been notified if within the standard blasting notification distance (typically 200+ feet depending on charge size). The pipeline operator must determine safe distances and charge limits based on pipeline depth, material, and proximity. Vibration monitoring should be conducted on the pipe or nearby - readings should not exceed pipeline design limits typically 2-4 inches per second peak particle velocity (PPV). Pre-blast and post-blast inspections must document coating and pipe condition. If the blasting is already underway without notification, immediate contact with the blasting contractor is required to understand blast schedule and review safety. If blasting is imminent, emergency hold may be needed to establish monitoring. For future blasts, pipeline operator should be present with vibration monitoring equipment. The blasting contractor must be educated on the pipeline location and depth per One Call requirements. If past blasting has occurred without monitoring, an integrity assessment may be needed including coating survey and potentially inline inspection to assess for damage. The pipeline company may require the blasting contractor to sign an agreement acknowledging the pipeline and safety requirements. If damage is discovered, the blasting contractor is liable for repair costs and DOT reporting may be required.
abnormal_variants: Ground heaving observed at pipe location

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline patrol observes a child playing on an exposed pipeline section in a rural area. The exposed section is approximately 6 feet long with 8 inches of pipe visible above ground. The coating is damaged and metal is visible. The child's home is 200 feet away and no adults are present.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: An exposed pipeline with exposed metal and a child playing nearby is an immediate safety concern per 49 CFR 192.703 and 192.617. The operator must first ensure the child is safely away from the exposed pipe - approach calmly and ask the child to move away. Do not attempt to physically restrain or scare the child. Once the child is clear, assess the situation. Exposed metal indicates coating damage and potential corrosion exposure. The location should be secured with temporary fencing or barriers until proper repair can be made. The landowner must be contacted and notified of the exposure and danger. Immediate temporary repair should include coating the exposed metal with tape or mastic to prevent corrosion. Permanent repair requires proper recoat and depth restoration. Depth of cover is inadequate - soil must be brought in to restore proper cover or the pipe must be lowered if settlement has occurred. The area must be evaluated for how the exposure occurred - washout from water, erosion, animal activity, or previous excavation are common causes. A cathodic protection reading should be taken at the exposure to verify the pipe remains adequately protected. If the pipe shows wall loss from corrosion, additional assessment may be needed including wall thickness measurement or inspection of adjacent sections. The repair must be completed within appropriate timeframes - typically temporary repair within days and permanent within months depending on severity and regulations. Documentation must include photos, measurements, landowner notification, and repair plans. If the exposure resulted from third-party activity, investigation and cost recovery may be pursued. The area should be added to increased patrol frequency until resolved.
abnormal_variants: Child refuses to leave the area

test_cases.scenario_description: A 12-inch pipeline requires a hot tap to install a pressure relief valve connection. The pipeline is on a slope with high point of 85 feet at the tap location. The operating pressure is 1440 psig (MAOP). The pipe is API 5L X65 with 0.500-inch wall.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping at a high point on a pipeline at MAOP requires careful planning per API 1104 and ASME B31.8. High points may have reduced flow conditions and increased temperature during welding. The slope creates potential for vapors to collect. The X65 high-strength material requires preheat to 300°F minimum for 0.500-inch wall at high pressure. The relief valve hot tap requires a full encirclement tee to distribute the stresses. The maximum allowable pressure during welding must be calculated for the X65 at the preheat temperature - this may require pressure reduction during the operation. At MAOP operations, pressure reduction to 80% MAOP (1150 psig) is often required for hot tapping. The weld procedure must be qualified for X65 with strict heat input control to under 50 kJ/inch to avoid HAZ softening. Interpass must not exceed 550°F. After welding, the coupon cutter must operate at low RPM to avoid work hardening the high-strength steel. The relief valve setting must be verified before installation - factory settings may need adjustment. The piping configuration must include proper drainage and blowdown capability. After installation, the relief valve must be tested per ASME Section VIII. The hot tap must be clearly marked and documented in station records. Given the MAOP operation, a pressure safety analysis should confirm the relief valve provides adequate protection.
abnormal_variants: Pressure cannot be reduced - must tap at MAOP
Hardness test shows 28 HRC in HAZ

material_control.description: X65 heat input and hardness control

test_cases.scenario_description: A technician must perform ultrasonic thickness testing inside a 36-inch pipeline. The section is 500 feet long between two block valves. The line has been isolated, purged with nitrogen, and depressurized. Entry is through the upstream block valve with the downstream valve closed.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Long-distance pipeline entry for ultrasonic testing requires specialized confined space procedures. At 500 feet from the entry point, standard rescue procedures may be inadequate - rescue planning must account for the distance and time. The pipeline must be fully ventilated with fresh air before entry - forced air ventilation must reach the remote end. Air quality testing should be conducted at multiple points along the 500-foot length. Communication is critical - hard-wire communication or radio repeaters may be needed as radio signals may not propagate the full length. The technician must wear a full-body harness with a retrieval line attached to a winch system capable of pulling the technician 500 feet. Entry must be in pairs - two technicians enter together maintaining visual contact. A rescue team trained in long-pipe rescue must be on standby with appropriate equipment. The technicians must carry portable gas monitors with alarms for O2, LEL, H2S, and CO. Escape breathing apparatus (EEBA) must be available at the entry point. The UT equipment must be intrinsically safe or used with hot work permits. Travel inside the pipe requires care - the surface may be slippery or have deposits. The work should be planned to minimize entry time - 500 feet of testing is significant and may require multiple entries. Lighting must be adequate for inspection - explosion-proof portable lights. If the pipe shows significant corrosion, the entry is even higher risk and additional precautions may be needed. After completion, the line must be re-purged if nitrogen atmosphere is needed before return to service.
abnormal_variants: Gas monitor alarms at remote location

test_cases.scenario_description: A 16-inch pipeline requires exposure in a residential area with high water table. The excavation is 8 feet deep and groundwater is expected at 4 feet. The soil is saturated clay. The excavation will be open for 3 days during coating repair.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Long-duration excavation in saturated clay requires comprehensive protective systems per OSHA 1926 Subpart P. Saturated clay is typically Type C soil with zero cohesion when saturated. A protective system is mandatory at 8 feet depth. The 3-day duration precludes simple sloping due to erosion and weather exposure. Trench shields or hydraulic shoring are required. Dewatering must be continuous to maintain stable working conditions - well points or sump pumps positioned outside the excavation. Dewatering discharge must be managed per EPA regulations - typically to a storm water system with sediment control. The trench must be inspected daily by a competent person and after any rain event which may worsen conditions. Access/egress requires a ladder within 25 feet - in wet conditions, the ladder must be secured. Atmospheric testing may be needed due to depth and potential for oxygen displacement in saturated soils. The residential area requires traffic control and neighbor notification. Spoil must be kept at least 2 feet from the edge - in wet conditions, greater distances may be needed to prevent surcharge loading. The saturated soil may slump - benching or wider shoring may be required. Pipeline support may be needed to prevent pipe movement during dewatering. After work completion, proper backfill and compaction are critical to prevent future settlement. Soil samples may be needed to confirm no contamination before disposal. If rain occurs during the work period, work may need to pause until the trench can be re-evaluated for stability.

protective_system.description: Trench protection for saturated Type C

dewatering.description: Well point or sump pump setup

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline requires annual valve exercise on a 60-inch mainline block valve with hydraulic actuator. The valve shows corrosion on the exterior and has not been fully stroked in 2 years. The valve is critical for emergency isolation.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Annual valve exercise on a large critical valve requires careful execution per 49 CFR 192.745. The exterior corrosion must be assessed before operation - if the valve body or bonnet shows significant section loss, exercise may be deferred pending inspection. Hydraulic actuator systems require checking hydraulic fluid levels and pressure before operation. The accumulator must be charged to proper pressure. Full stroking means opening a closed valve or closing an open valve completely, then returning to original position.
