The critical nature means any malfunction would be serious - pre-exercise testing should include partial stroke (10-20%) to verify operation before full stroke. Current and pressure readings on the hydraulic system should be monitored - high current indicates resistance or binding. If the valve does not reach full open/close position within expected time, it should be stopped and investigated. Position limit switches must be verified for proper adjustment. Torque or pressure settings should not be exceeded to force operation. After exercise, the valve must be returned to normal operating position and verified at SCADA. Exercise time should be documented - valves over 36 inches typically take 1-2 minutes per inch. During exercise, pipeline flow should be monitored for impacts. The critical classification of this valve means any issues require immediate response - spare parts and technical support should be available before starting. If exercise reveals problems, a condition assessment and repair plan must be developed before the valve is needed for emergency service. Corrosion found during exercise should trigger coating repair and possibly structural assessment.
abnormal_variants: Position switch fails to indicate closure

technique.description: Exercise procedure and partial stroke test

test_cases.scenario_description: A reported gas leak investigation at a residence finds no leak on the house piping but detects 15% LEL in the crawl space. The crawl space is vented to atmosphere. The gas service line is 20 feet long from the meter to the house.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Gas accumulation in a crawl space without an apparent leak source requires systematic investigation per 49 CFR 192.615. The service line from the meter to the house must be leak tested including underground sections. A possible cause is a leak in the underground service line allowing gas to migrate through soil into the crawl space. The soil type affects migration - sandy soils allow more migration than clay. The service line should be pressure tested to verify integrity. If the service line tests tight, other sources must be considered including: migration from a nearby main leak, abandoned service lines, or sewer line leaks carrying gas. The crawl space must be ventilated to clear the gas before any ignition source work. The customer should be advised to avoid electrical switching and other ignition sources until cleared. If gas continues to accumulate after ventilation, ongoing migration is occurring. Excavation of the service line may be needed to locate the leak. All work must follow 49 CFR 192.605 procedures. If a leak is found, repair requires proper materials and techniques. The house should be monitored after repair to confirm no further accumulation. The crawl space may need improved ventilation to prevent future accumulation. Documentation must include leak test results, investigation findings, and repair records. If migration from another source is suspected, that source must be located and addressed. The incident may indicate a need for patrol of the area to identify other possible leak sources.
abnormal_variants: Leak located at gas main 200 feet away
Multiple houses show crawl space accumulation
Repair requires hot work in crawl space

test_cases.scenario_description: A car has crashed into a pipeline valve station building, rupturing a 3-inch instrument air line and causing a fire. The driver is trapped in the vehicle. The building contains station isolation valves for the mainline.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A vehicle crash into a valve station with fire and trapped driver is a complex emergency per 49 CFR 192.615. First priorities are driver rescue and preventing escalation. The station ESD should be activated to isolate the pipeline and depressurize station piping to reduce fuel supply to the fire. Fire department must be notified with details including the trapped driver and facility hazards. Personnel must not attempt vehicle extraction without proper equipment and fire protection. The fire department will have extrication tools and can provide cover during rescue. If station isolation valves are accessible, the mainline should be isolated to prevent potential larger release. Firefighters must be informed of gas hazards and valve locations. Vehicle fuel and possible pipeline contents are fueling the fire. Water can be used if safe - but electrical systems must be isolated first. Once the driver is rescued and fire contained, the primary pipeline system must be evaluated for integrity. The instrument air line rupture may affect valve operation - manual operation capability must be verified. If the valve building is damaged, temporary protection of valves may be needed. The station ESD valves should be verified as closed. The mainline must be leak surveyed before restart. The damaged building must be secured. DOT reporting is required for fire and damage to pipeline facilities per 49 CFR 191.5. Investigation must determine if additional vehicle barriers are needed at the station. The driver may have also struck the mainline - the pipe must be inspected for damage.

rescue.description: Driver extraction coordination with fire dept

test_cases.scenario_description: A facility is performing catalyst change-out in a vessel. The vessel contained sour gas treating solution and residual H2S is being released during the opening. The entry team is wearing SCBA and full PPE but a supervisor without respirator approaches to check progress.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Unauthorized approach to an H2S release area by unprotected personnel is a serious violation per API RP 55 and OSHA respiratory protection standards. The supervisor must be immediately removed from the area and educated on the hazard. Even momentary exposure to high H2S concentrations can be fatal. The vessel entry must be evaluated - if the supervisor was exposed, medical evaluation is required. The entry should be paused until the situation is controlled. A proper hot work or vessel entry permit should have established the H2S hazard zone and required respiratory protection within that zone. The supervisor's action indicates a breakdown in communication and controls - all personnel should have been briefed on the H2S hazard and required PPE. The access to the area should be controlled with barriers and signage. A safety watch should be posted to prevent unprotected entry. The vessel entry team should have a dedicated safety attendant in communication who can direct unprotected personnel away. The H2S levels at the entry point must be monitored continuously. If the vessel opening is releasing H2S, the area should be cordoned and only protected entry permitted. The work should be evaluated to determine if additional engineering controls like local exhaust ventilation can reduce emissions. The entry team should be debriefed to ensure they understand the importance of controlling access. The incident should be documented as a safety violation and refresher training conducted for all facility personnel. H2S safety is life-critical and violations cannot be tolerated.
abnormal_variants: H2S spike to 100 ppm during incident

immediate_response.description: Remove unprotected person and medical eval

access_control.description: Establish H2S zone barriers and signage

communication.description: Brief all personnel on PPE requirements

test_cases.scenario_description: A gas storage field wellhead is showing pressure decline of 50 psi per day when the field should be in injection mode. The well has been injecting for 3 weeks. Other wells show normal injection pressures.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Unexpected pressure decline during injection suggests a well leak or formation issue per 49 CFR 192.617 and API RP 1114. The specific well showing decline while others are normal indicates wellbore or near-wellbore issues. Injection should be stopped on the affected well to prevent further loss and assess the situation. The well must be isolated at the wellhead valves. The casing annulus pressure should be checked - rising casing pressure indicates tubing or packer failure allowing gas into the annulus. The wellhead should be leak tested with soapy water and gas detection. Wellhead connections, flanges, and valves should be inspected for visible leaks. If wellhead is tight, the issue may be downhole - tubing leak, packer failure, or formation fracture propagation. Downhole issues require well intervention with a workover rig. The injection volume lost must be calculated - reportable quantities may apply. The storage field integrity must be evaluated - if injection is going out of zone, environmental impact must be assessed. State oil and gas regulators must be notified of the storage well issue. If the well cannot hold pressure, it may need to be plugged or repaired. Nearby water wells should be monitored if injection is near potable water zones. The well should not be returned to service until the cause is identified and corrected. Injection to other wells may continue if they are holding pressure normally.
abnormal_variants: Annulus pressure rises to injection pressure
Gas detected at nearby water well

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline company discovers that a power utility has installed new 69kV power lines crossing the pipeline right-of-way. The poles are 45 feet from the pipeline centerline and the lines cross at 35 feet height. No notification was made to the pipeline operator.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Unauthorized power line installation near a pipeline is an encroachment requiring immediate assessment per 49 CFR 192.614 and electrical safety standards. Power lines 69kV create electrical induction hazards on the pipeline - AC interference can cause corrosion, shock hazards, and coating damage. The proximity of 45 feet is within the pipeline AC interference zone. The pipeline company must assess the AC interference levels - pipe-to-soil potentials should be measured under the power lines. AC voltage on the pipe must be below 15V per safety limits. If AC interference is present, AC mitigation (grounding or isolation) is required. The power company must be contacted to review their installation and notification requirements. Many states require notification for crossings - failure to notify is a violation. The pipeline may need to be relocated or power lines re-routed if interference cannot be mitigated. Inspected for damage from construction - coating, cathodic protection, and physical damage to the pipe. The power pole excavation should be hand-exposed to verify depth and any pipe contact. Documentation includes survey of the crossing, AC interference measurements, and correspondence with the power utility. If AC interference exceeds acceptable levels, remedial action is needed before the lines are energized. NACE SP0177 provides guidance on AC interference mitigation. The power company may be liable for mitigation costs. If the power lines were energized without assessment, emergency measures may be needed to de-energize until safe.
abnormal_variants: Power line droops closer to ground
Pipeline coating damaged from pole installation

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline marker post has been hit and destroyed by agricultural equipment. The operator discovers the incident 3 weeks after it occurred based on a patrol finding. The area shows recent deep tilling over the pipeline.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Discovery of a destroyed marker post with evidence of agricultural activity indicates a potential unreported encroachment per 49 CFR 192.707. The lack of notification for deep tilling in the right-of-way is a serious violation. Immediate actions include locating and marking the pipeline in the area and surveying for damage. Hand-digging is required to verify pipe depth and inspect for coating damage. Cathodic protection readings must be taken to verify pipe condition. The farmer must be contacted to learn the extent of tilling and confirm whether the pipeline was struck. Deep tilling equipment can contact and damage pipelines. If damage is found, repair costs may be recovered from the farmer. The farmer must be educated on One Call requirements for any future work. A new marker post must be installed per DOT specifications - typically visible from the right-of-way with operator contact information. The area should be added to increased patrol frequency. If the pipe was contacted, additional integrity assessment may be needed including wall thickness measurement or inline inspection if available. Documentation must include photographs, farmer contact information, damage assessment, and repair records. If damage is significant, DOT reporting may be required per 49 CFR 191.5. The incident should be reported to the state One Call center for awareness. Legal action may be necessary if the farmer refuses to cooperate or has a pattern of violations.

investigation.description: Locate, mark, and inspect for damage

test_cases.scenario_description: A 30-inch pipeline with internal epoxy coating requires a hot tap to install a smart pig receiver. The coating is 12 mils thick and abrasion resistant. The pipe is API 5L X52 with 0.375-inch wall at 500 psig.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping an internally coated pipeline requires consideration of coating damage from welding and cutting per API 1104 and coating manufacturer guidelines. The epoxy coating will be damaged where the tee welds to the pipe and where the coupon is cut. The internal coating damage cannot be repaired without accessing the interior, which requires taking the line out of service. Therefore, hot tapping internally coated lines is often limited to situations where coating damage is acceptable or the location is already planned for recoating. The welding arc will burn back approximately 1-2 inches of coating from the weld toe. This creates a potential corrosion initiation point. The hot tap procedure must acknowledge the coating damage and plan for future recoating if needed. The coupon cutter must be selected to minimize coating damage - some cutters are designed to minimize coating peel-back. The tee must be sized to ensure the hot tap hole is clear of the original pipe walls. Preheat requirements for X52 at 0.375-inch wall are typically 150-200°F. The hot tap location should avoid areas where internal corrosion has been detected - UT inspection should confirm wall thickness. After installation, the coating around the hot tap can be visually inspected with a borescope if available. The damaged coating area should be documented for future integrity assessment. The smart pig receiver installation on an internally coated line means the receiver barrel will also be internally coated. The receiver must be coated with compatible material and proper surface preparation. The hot tap connection must be electrically isolated from the pipeline if the receiver is isolating - bonding jumpers may be needed. Post-installation, pigging should confirm no coating debris was introduced. The receiver must be rated for pigging forces.
abnormal_variants: Coating delamination extends beyond weld zone

welding_procedure.description: Heat input and coating burn-back control

cutting.description: Coupon cutter selection for minimal damage

test_cases.scenario_description: A technician must enter a wastewater sump adjacent to a compressor station to repair a leaking level transmitter. The sump is 15 feet deep 8-foot diameter with 2 feet of water and sludge at the bottom. H2S levels are detected at 15 ppm at the entry hatch.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Entry into a wastewater sump with elevated H2S requires enhanced confined space procedures per OSHA 1910.146 and API RP 55. At 15 ppm, entry requires supplied air respirators or SCBA - standard H2S cartridges are insufficient for entry at this level. The H2S source must be evaluated - wastewater with organic material commonly produces H2S. Continuous ventilation using explosion-proof blowers must be established to reduce H2S, but entry cannot proceed until levels drop below 10 ppm or proper respiratory protection is used. The sludge at the bottom may harbor higher H2S concentrations - gas readings should be taken at multiple depths. The water level creates drowning hazard - life preservers or safety lines are required for work near the water. The technician must wear full-body harness with retrieval line for confined space rescue. A tripod and winch rated for the entry must be set up. The attendant must maintain visual and voice contact and is responsible for retrieval if needed. Communication must cover the rescue plan - sump rescues are difficult due to depth and configuration. PPE includes chemical-resistant clothing for wastewater exposure. The work involves electrical equipment in a wet environment - the level transmitter must be properly disconnected and locked out before work. If the repair requires hot work, additional permits are required. Non-sparking tools must be used. After repair, the area must be cleaned and disinfected. The H2S monitoring indicates poor ventilation in the sump - evaluation of the vent system should be part of the scope. Sludge disposal is hazardous waste requiring proper manifest per EPA. Entry cannot proceed until rescue equipment is tested and ready.
abnormal_variants: H2S increases to 35 ppm during pump operation
Entrant shows H2S symptoms during work

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline requires emergency repair in a wetlands area. The excavation is 6 feet deep in saturated peat soil. The wetland is classified as protected and requires minimal disturbance. Work must be completed within 48 hours to restore flow.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Emergency excavation in protected wetlands requires balancing pipeline repair with environmental protection per OSHA 1926 Subpart P and EPA Clean Water Act Section 404. Wetlands excavation in peat presents stability issues - peat is typically Type C soil with very low bearing capacity. Protective systems must account for unstable ground - conventional shoring may sink. The wetland classification determines permit requirements - some emergency repairs have permit exemptions but restoration requirements still apply. The excavation must minimize the footprint - work from one side if possible. Dewatering in wetlands requires sediment control - water must be treated before discharge. The peat soil when excavated loses strength quickly - sloping sidewalls may slump. Saturated peat excavations should use light equipment or mats to distribute loads. The 48-hour timeline requires efficient work but safety cannot be compromised. Entry requires atmospheric testing and trench protection. The repair crew should be large enough to complete work without rushing safety procedures. Wetland restoration must be planned including stockpiling topsoil, preserving vegetation, and restoration of hydrology. The excavation should be backfilled with native material immediately after repair. Soil compaction must be minimal to preserve wetland function. If the repair requires multiple days, the excavation may need protection from rain and water accumulation. State environmental agencies should be notified of the emergency repair. Documentation must include before/during/after photos and restoration plan.

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline has detected an anomaly requiring immediate inspection at a remote valve location accessible only by helicopter. The valve is at 8,000 feet elevation in mountainous terrain. Weather is marginal with cloud ceiling at 9,000 feet.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Valve operations at high-altitude remote locations by helicopter require specialized planning per 49 CFR 192.745 and aviation safety protocols. Access by helicopter adds aviation hazards to pipeline operations. Weather minimums must be strictly observed - marginal conditions with low ceiling are unsafe for helicopter operations. If the ceiling is truly 9,000 feet, operations at 8,000 feet may be feasible but requires pilot judgment. The valve operation must be critical to justify helicopter operations - if safety is not immediately threatened, waiting for better weather or ground access may be preferred. Personnel must be trained in helicopter safety including approach/departure zones, rotor hazard, and communications with pilot. Equipment must be secured for transport - tools can become projectiles in rotor wash. Fuel for the helicopter requires special handling and fire safety. The valve operation itself must be planned efficiently to minimize helicopter loiter time. Multiple tasks should be combined if possible. The operator should bring redundant equipment in case of primary tool failure - returning for missing tools is costly and may not be possible if weather deteriorates. If the valve cannot be operated successfully with available tools, a contingency plan is needed. Emergency response must be considered - medical evacuation from a remote mountain site takes significant time. After operations, the pilot must confirm weather on return route. If weather closes in, personnel could be stranded at the site. Communication via satellite phone or radio is essential. Documentation includes flight manifest, weather assessment, and operation time.

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline control room receives multiple leak detection system alarms on a single pipeline section. The alarms indicate a sudden flow imbalance and pressure drop. Estimated release volume is 50,000 cubic feet in 10 minutes.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Multiple leak detection alarms with significant calculated release requires immediate emergency response per 49 CFR 192.615. The combination of flow imbalance and pressure drop suggests a rupture or major leak. Immediate dispatch to the location must include emergency response personnel with gas detection. The control room should begin valve closure to isolate the affected section - upstream and downstream block valves should be commanded closed. Customer notifications must begin if the section supplies end users. The dispatch should identify access routes and potential ignition sources near the alarms. SCADA should be reviewed to identify the exact alarm location for dispatch. Public safety officials must be notified - fire and police dispatch with approximate location and gas hazard. The estimated release of 50,000 cubic feet in 10 minutes is significant - evacuation zones should be established based on ERG tables, typically 300-500 feet minimum. Initial responders should approach from upwind and verify gas presence before entry. If gas is confirmed and readings are high, expand the safety zone. The control room must track valve closures to confirm isolation. If valves fail to close, dispatch should be notified to prepare manual closure. The leak location must be pinpointed once on scene using gas detection, visual observation, and acoustic methods. If the leak is on the pipeline, further isolation may be needed from adjacent valves. After isolation, remaining gas should be calculated to determine total release volume. Environmental assessment should identify sensitive areas downwind. Media may respond - PIO should be activated. DOT notification must occur within required timeframes. The cause must be investigated including inline inspection data review if available.
abnormal_variants: Leak detection shows multiple locations - system malfunction

test_cases.scenario_description: A lightning strike has caused a fire in the control building electronics room. Smoke is visible. The fire is isolated to the electronics cabinets. The pipeline SCADA system is still operational but at risk.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Fire in a control building with critical SCADA equipment requires immediate response to protect pipeline operations per NFPA 75 and facility emergency plans. Electronics fires involving energized equipment and plastics require careful suppression agent selection. Water is not appropriate for energized electrical fires - CO2, clean agent, or dry chemical should be used. The fire must be suppressed quickly to prevent SCADA system failure. If suppression cannot be safely attempted, evacuation and fire department notification are priorities. Power to the affected cabinets should be switched off remotely or at the panel if safe to do so - this reduces both the fire and shock hazards. The control room operators should prepare for potential SCADA failure - identify manual backup procedures and communication methods. If SCADA fails during suppression, the pipeline may continue operating but with reduced visibility and control. The fire department should be notified of the fire location and the critical nature of the equipment - if using water, confirm power is off first. After suppression, the electronics room must be ventilated before entry due to smoke and potential toxic gases from burning plastics. The SCADA equipment must be assessed for damage before restart. Redundant systems or backup control centers may need activation. The smoke detection and suppression systems should be inspected. The root cause of ignition should be determined - lightning protection should be evaluated. The incident should be documented including equipment damage and service interruption. Insurance claims may apply. Temporary SCADA restoration or manual operation plans should be prepared.

test_cases.scenario_description: A routine pig trap operation releases residual sour gas when the closure is opened. The operator detects H2S at 35 ppm at the closure door. The pig trap is in a congested processing area between equipment.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: H2S release at 35 ppm during routine pig trap operations requires immediate response and procedure review per API RP 55. At 35 ppm, entry requires supplied air or escape is mandatory - this exceeds cartridge limits. Personnel must evacuate immediately upwind from the release point. The closure door should be secured in a position that minimizes gas release if safe to do so, or evacuated and remotely monitored. Fresh air ventilation should be directed at the trap if equipment is available. The release indicates inadequate purging or draining before opening - procedures should suspend the operation until the cause is identified. The pig may contain fluids that are off-gassing H2S. Before re-entry, the area should be monitored until H2S drops below 10 ppm or SCBA should be donned. The operation should be reviewed to improve purge procedures - nitrogen or air purging may need to be extended. Draining liquids before opening may be required. The pig receiver design may need evaluation - some designs have better venting capabilities. Personal H2S monitors should alarm at 10 ppm giving notice before reaching 35 ppm. If alarms were delayed, equipment calibration should be checked. The area around the trap should be evaluated for wind patterns that trap gas - congestion creates dead zones. Modifying procedures or adding ventilation may be needed for future operations. Medical surveillance is required for anyone exposed above 10 ppm. Work cannot continue until H2S is controlled and procedures revised.
abnormal_variants: Trap contains liquid slug releasing H2S

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline SCADA system indicates a station has lost communications. The last data shows normal operations 4 hours ago. The station is a critical compressor facility with redundancy.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Loss of SCADA communications to a critical compressor station is a serious abnormal condition per 49 CFR 192.617 and 192.615. The 4-hour duration is significant - the station may be operating normally but without visibility, or may have had an incident. Immediate actions include dispatching personnel to the site to assess conditions. Communications should be attempted via backup methods - satellite phone, radio, or telephone if available. The control room should attempt to communicate with any personnel at the station. If communications cannot be established, a higher urgency dispatch may be needed. The station design should have fail-safe logic - if SCADA comms are lost, the station may automatically shut down or continue at last setpoint depending on design. The control room should identify if any alarms were missed during the 4-hour gap - data historians may have recorded events even if real-time display failed. Safety systems may have activated without notification. Dispatch arriving on site should first assess for hazardous conditions before approaching. Signs of fire, gas release, or emergency should be observable from a distance. If the station is operating normally, backup communication should be established and the primary SCADA issue diagnosed. If the station has shut down, restart can proceed once comms are restored. If an incident is discovered, emergency response procedures apply immediately. The communication system failure should be investigated - redundant paths should not fail simultaneously indicating a common cause. Documentation must capture the 4-hour blind period and any consequential events.
abnormal_variants: Personnel at station also unable to communicate

test_cases.scenario_description: A railway company is upgrading track across the pipeline right-of-way. The existing railway is being elevated by 2 feet requiring extensive excavation and new track ballast. The pipe is 36 inches at 48-inch depth under the existing track.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Railway track elevation over a pipeline requires careful planning per 49 CFR 192.614 and AREMA guidelines. Excavation under a railroad requires specific protective systems - trench shields designed for railroad surcharge loading. The work must be scheduled during track windows if active. The existing track must be supported during excavation - temporary shoring or bridging is required. The pipe exposure must be hand-dug within the tolerance zone - 24 inches laterally at minimum. The pipe must be protected from excavation equipment - concrete barrier or padding may be required. The new ballast must not be compacted directly on unprotected pipe - proper backfill compacted to specifications first. The increased elevation adds soil load on the pipe - the pipe must be evaluated for additional cover load. If cover depth will exceed design limits, pipe stress must be analyzed. Cathodic protection must be maintained - bonding jumpers may be needed for track welding. The railway work must not damage the pipe through vibration or load - track maintenance equipment must stay clear. Pre and post-construction pipe inspection including coating survey and wall thickness should be conducted. If coating damage occurs, immediate repair is required. The railway company is typically responsible for coordination but the pipeline operator must protect their interests. Long-term monitoring may be needed for settlement. Documentation includes before/after surveys and load calculations.

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline patrol discovers construction has begun on a high-rise building within the pipeline right-of-way. The foundation excavation shows 30-foot deep pilings proposed at 40 feet from the pipeline centerline. The construction company claims they have city permits.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Unauthorized high-rise construction near a pipeline is a serious high consequence area (HCA) issue per 49 CFR 192.614 and HCA definitions. The construction significantly increases the population density and encroaches the easement. The 30-foot deep excavation 40 feet from the pipe is within influence zone - settlement from piling could affect the pipe. The pipeline must be located and marked immediately. Hand-dug verification may be required to confirm depth and establish clearance requirements. The construction is creating an HCA where one may not have existed - pipeline integrity management requirements may change. The foundation piling vibration may affect the pipe - vibration monitoring may be required. The city permits do not override pipeline easement rights - legal review of the permits and easement is needed. If construction proceeds, protective measures including: pipe support during excavation, vibration limits, settlement monitoring, and possibly pipe relocation must be considered. The building occupancy will create an HCA requiring enhanced integrity measures. If the construction cannot be legally stopped, the pipeline may need relocation at the developer's expense. Public safety officials should be notified of the hazard. PHMSA may need notification of the HCA change. Construction damage prevention requirements apply - the developer should have notified the One Call system. The pipeline company should document all interactions and damages for potential legal action.

test_cases.scenario_description: A 24-inch pipeline containing high-viscosity crude oil at 180°F requires hot tapping. The oil has pour point of 50°F and requires heating to maintain flow. The pipe is API 5L X56 with 0.312-inch wall.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Hot tapping high-viscosity heated crude oil requires special procedures per API 1104 and API 2201. The operating temperature of 180°F affects material properties and preheat requirements. At this temperature, standard preheat may already be satisfied but heat removal during welding could cause localized cooling which might allow the viscous oil to gel at the weld. The coupon cutter must be rated for elevated temperature - seals and materials must handle 180°F. Thermal expansion of the pipe at operating temperature must be considered when positioning the hot tap tee - compensation for thermal movement may be needed. The high viscosity means the pipe is acting as a heat exchanger - any shutdown or flow reduction during hot tapping could cause wax deposition and plugging. The weld procedure must account for the elevated temperature - interpass temperature should not exceed 500°F. The hot tap machine body will be heated by conduction - personnel must use appropriate PPE for hot surfaces. The coupon when cut may be hot - handling precautions required. Coating around the hot tap location may be softened by the operating temperature - insulation or heat sinks may be needed to protect the coating. The hot tap must not damage the heating system (steam tracing or electrical) if present. After installation, the tee must allow for thermal expansion - stress analysis may indicate need for expansion loops or flexible connections. Material selection for the tee must be compatible with the X56 pipe at operating temperature. Post-weld inspection must verify no cracks from thermal cycling.
abnormal_variants: Oil gels at coupon cutter location

temperature_management.description: Preheat, heat sink, and thermal expansion

test_cases.scenario_description: A crew must inspect the interior of a large underground fuel storage tank. The tank is 50 feet diameter and 30 feet high with a single 36-inch manway. The tank was emptied and cleaned 2 weeks ago but residual vapors may remain.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Entry into a large underground fuel storage tank requires comprehensive confined space procedures per OSHA 1910.146 and API 653. Even though emptied and cleaned, residual hydrocarbon vapors remain and the tank atmosphere must be tested. Testing must occur at multiple locations throughout the tank volume - fuel vapors are heavier than air and concentrate at the bottom. Initial readings of O2, LEL, H2S, and benzene must be acceptable before entry. Given the size, forced air ventilation is required - calculated at 20 air changes per hour for this volume. Ventilation blowers must be explosion-proof. Entry is permit-required - the permit must specify entry team, hazards, controls, and emergency contacts. Entry should be in pairs using buddy system. Full-body harness with retrieval lines attached to tripod and winch are required. Winch must be capable of lifting workers from the bottom. Communication via radio or hard-wire is essential. Lighting must be explosion-proof. Non-sparking tools required. If the tank had any sludge, it may be pyrophoric - wet down before disturbance. Entry team must have escape breathing apparatus. Attendant must stay at manway with direct communication. Rescue planning is critical given depth - a vertical rescue will be needed if worker becomes disabled. Rescue team should be on standby during entry. Tank walls must be inspected for corrosion, coating condition, and structural integrity. Documentation of findings required. If repairs are needed, hot work permits will be required for subsequent entry.
abnormal_variants: High benzene detected in tank atmosphere

ventilation.description: Calculated air changes and blower placement

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline company must excavate a section of buried pipeline in permafrost terrain. The excavation is 10 feet deep in ice-rich soil. Ambient temperature is 20°F. The pipe is 36 inches at 8 feet depth.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Excavation in permafrost requires special geotechnical considerations per OSHA 1926 Subpart P and environmental regulations. Ice-rich permafrost is unstable when thawed - excavation exposes frozen soil to warm air causing rapid thawing and loss of bearing capacity. The excavation sidewalls may slump as ice melts. Protective systems must account for frozen soil behavior - conventional trench boxes may not be suitable. Engineered solutions may include: maintaining frozen conditions using refrigeration, allowing controlled thawing with temporary support, or using specialized shoring. Dewatering from melting ice can saturate the excavation. The frozen soil contains ice that expands and contracts - excavation should minimize exposure time. Backfill must allow for refreezing without damaging the pipe - proper material selection and compaction. If the pipe was supported by frozen soil, thawing may cause settlement and stress. Pre-excavation ground temperature monitoring helps assess conditions. The excavation may expose ice wedges that accelerate thaw. At 20°F ambient, rapid thaw may not be a concern but equipment heat and solar exposure can still affect the frozen sidewalls. Pipeline damage in permafrost often occurs from frost heave or thaw settlement - the excavation should evaluate pipe movement. Cathodic protection systems must be assessed for freeze protection. Restoration must include proper insulation and backfill to restore thermal conditions. State regulations for permafrost disturbance may apply.
abnormal_variants: Excavation sidewalls slump due to thaw

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline requires valve alignment for maintenance requiring complete depressurization of a 50-mile section. The section contains two mainline block valves with 12 intermediate sectionalizing valves. Estimated gas volume is 120 MMSCF.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Coordinated valve alignment for depressurization requires systematic operation per 49 CFR 192.745 and pipeline operating procedures. All valves in the section must be verified operable before starting. The sequence should depressurize in a controlled manner to prevent equipment damage and manage customer impact. Typically the sequence opens all sectionalizing valves first to equalize pressure, then closes block valves at each end while maintaining vent paths. However, specific procedures must be followed. Intermediate valves may need to be positioned to facilitate venting - some may need to be partially open to route gas to vent locations. Customer notifications must precede any service interruption. As valves are operated, pressure and flow should be monitored at multiple points. Unexpected pressure differentials indicate valve leakage or improper positioning. After the section is isolated at both ends, the remaining gas must be vented or transferred. Venting should be to approved locations away from ignition sources and populated areas. The 120 MMSCF represents significant volume - environmental permits may apply for venting. If venting is prohibited, gas displacement with nitrogen may be required. As pressure drops, valve seats may leak differently - some valves seal better at lower pressure. Valve position verification is critical - each valve must be locally confirmed and documented. After depressurization, the section must be monitored for pressure buildup indicating leakage. The depressurization time should be recorded. Documentation must include position of every valve before and after. Before return to service, valves may need to be returned to normal positions.
abnormal_variants: Venting causes ice formation in pipe
Pressure does not drop at expected rate

test_cases.scenario_description: A farmer reports hitting something while deep plowing and now smells gas. The location is in a rural area 400 feet from the nearest residence. The farmer is continuing to work the field.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A reported strike by agricultural equipment with gas odor requires immediate response per 49 CFR 192.615 and 192.614. The first priority is stopping the farmer from continuing work that may worsen the damage or create ignition sources. Dispatch must contact the farmer and instruct them to immediately cease operations, shut off equipment, and evacuate away from the area. The farmer must be told not to start vehicles or use electrical equipment in the area. Dispatch must then send emergency responders with gas detection equipment. The pipeline must be located and marked if not already. Upon arrival, the area must be secured and the farmer interviewed to determine strike location and equipment used. Gas detection must identify the leak point and establish the hot zone. If gas readings indicate hazardous concentrations near residences, evacuation should be ordered. The damaged section must be isolated using the nearest block valves. For agricultural strikes, the equipment may have contacted the pipeline directly or damaged coating. If coating is damaged, the pipe integrity must be assessed - wall thickness measurement and direct inspection. Cathodic protection must be checked. If the pipe is leaking, repair procedures apply. If only coating is damaged, repair and soil sampling may be required. The farmer must be educated on One Call requirements - agricultural strikes should have been preceded by notification. Damage costs may be recovered from the farmer. Documentation must include location, equipment type, damage assessment, and photos. The area must be secured until repairs are complete. After repair, the pipe may need inline inspection or pressure testing depending on severity.
abnormal_variants: Pipeline is ruptured with large release

test_cases.scenario_description: A vehicle has crashed into a regulator station creating a gas release and fire at the meter set. The vehicle is on fire and the driver is trapped. The regulator station serves 500 customers. The fire is impinging on the building structure.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A vehicle crash with fire at a regulator station combines multiple hazards - fire, gas release, and rescue per 49 CFR 192.615. Immediate priorities are driver rescue and preventing gas-fed fire escalation. Fire department must be notified immediately with details: trapped driver, gas release, and station function. The station gas supply must be isolated - typically the upstream station valve or curb valve if accessible. If station isolation is not possible remotely, manual closure after fire department arrives may be an option. The fire department must be briefed that this is a gas fire - standard tactics apply but they must be aware of potential for major gas release if piping fails. Personnel must not attempt driver rescue without proper equipment - wait for fire department with extrication tools and PPE. The fire impinging on the building threatens structure collapse - all personnel should stay clear of collapse zones. The 500 customer service interruption must be evaluated - customer notification should begin once scene is secure. If the fire cannot be controlled, expand the evacuation zone. The vehicle fuel and gas release combine for a significant fire - water may be appropriate if gas supply is isolated. Firefighter safety is paramount - they must know the station layout and valve locations. After fire control, the driver must receive medical care. The station must be inspected before service restoration - piping may be damaged, regulators destroyed, and structure compromised. All customers must be notified of service interruption. Before gas restoration, repairs must be made and pressure testing conducted. The cause must be investigated including driver condition and any barriers that failed.
abnormal_variants: Main gas line damaged by crash

rescue.description: Coordinated driver extraction with fire dept

test_cases.scenario_description: A sour gas wellhead is being worked on by contractors. The work involves replacing a pressure gauge. The contractors are not company employees and do not have H2S training. The well contains gas with 1000 ppm H2S.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Contractor work on sour gas equipment without H2S training is a serious violation that must be stopped per API RP 55 and OSHA worker protection. The work must be immediately halted until all personnel have H2S awareness training. The contractor company must provide evidence of H2S training for each worker before proceeding. Personnel must demonstrate competency including: H2S properties and hazards, detection and alarm systems, respiratory protection use, and emergency response. If they lack training, on-site training must be provided by qualified instructors before work continues. At 1000 ppm H2S, the well is deadly - a single breath can be fatal. All personnel must have SCBA or at minimum EEBA available during work. The company must verify contractors have the required PPE and are using it properly. A safety watch trained in H2S must be present during work. The well work permit must reflect the H2S hazard and confirm contractor qualifications. Access to the area must be controlled with barriers and signage warning of H2S. Contractors should not work alone - buddy system required. If the contractor cannot demonstrate adequate training and PPE, a different contractor should be assigned or the work postponed. Medical surveillance requirements for H2S exposure must be explained. The incident of unauthorized work should be reported and preventive measures implemented including pre-job verification of contractor qualifications.
abnormal_variants: H2S release occurs during unauthorized work
Contractor claims prior H2S training but cannot document

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline SCADA system shows increasing flow rate with steady pressure at a custody transfer station. The flow rate is 15% above contracted amount. The downstream customer denies taking excess gas.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Unexplained flow increase at a custody transfer station indicates measurement error, equipment malfunction, or unaccounted flow per API MPMS and custody transfer agreements. Initial investigation must verify if the flow increase is actual or instrument error. Local flow meter readings and calculations should be checked against SCADA. The meter type determines diagnostic approach - differential pressure meters may have impulse line issues, ultrasonic meters may have signal problems, turbine meters may have bearing wear. The flow computer must be checked for proper configuration and alarming limits. If the meter shows actual flow, investigation must find the destination - the gas is going somewhere. Potential causes include: bypass around the meter, misaligned valves directing flow, downstream system leak, or theft. The custody transfer station piping should be inspected for bypass connections. Valves around the meter should be checked for position and isolation integrity. Downstream pressure analysis may show where the gas is going. The customer may have undisclosed use - contractual implications if they are exceeding contracted volumes. If theft is suspected, legal and security should be involved. The measurement discrepancy affects both parties - financial settlement may be impacted. Temporary backup measurement may be needed while the primary meter is investigated. If the issue is instrument error, calibration or repair is needed. Documentation of the discrepancy and investigation is required for custody transfer records. Until resolved, conservative measurement approaches should be used.

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline operator discovers that a developer has sold pipeline easement property to multiple buyers as residential lots. The pipeline is a 30-inch transmission in the center of a new subdivision. No pipeline markers are present and buyers are unaware of the pipeline.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: Discovery of unmarked transmission pipeline in a residential subdivision is a critical new high consequence area (HCA) requiring immediate action per 49 CFR 192.903, 192.935, and public safety requirements. The lack of awareness and markers creates imminent public safety risk. Immediate actions include installing pipeline markers per 192.707 requirements - visible with emergency contacts. The developer must be contacted and informed of easement restrictions and notification requirements. A survey should confirm exact pipeline location through the subdivision. Buyer notification must be made of the pipeline presence and safety requirements - typically through certified mail or recorded documents. The subdivision likely was required to notify One Call during construction - failure to do so should be reported. The integrity management program must be updated to reflect the new Class 3 or Class 4 HCA. Additional integrity assessment is required - inline inspection or pressure testing may be needed. Patrolling frequency should increase. The developer may be liable for damage prevention violations and costs to remediate. Legal action may be required to enforce easement rights. Public meetings may be needed to educate residents. The zoning authority should be notified of the encroachment for future development review. PHMSA notification of HCA change is required. The incident demonstrates a systemic failure of notification systems - the development was approved without pipeline awareness. Working with local authorities to improve development review processes may prevent recurrence. Long-term, the operator may need to consider pipe replacement with additional depth if feasible or enhanced monitoring.
abnormal_variants: Developer already sold all lots - no recourse

test_cases.scenario_description: A pipeline company is responding to a reported gas leak at a hospital emergency room. The odor is strong inside the ER which is at full capacity with 40 patients. The pipeline runs 75 feet from the building and recent utility work has been performed nearby.
test_cases.expected_response_detailed: A gas leak at a hospital ER is a mass casualty potential event requiring immediate action per 49 CFR 192.615. Hospital patients may be non-ambulatory and require specialized evacuation. First priorities are leak assessment and patient safety. Dispatch must respond immediately with gas detection equipment to locate the source. The hospital must be notified to prepare for potential evacuation - their emergency management plan should be activated. Gas readings inside the ER must be taken - if readings exceed 20% LEL, immediate evacuation is required. Evacuation of non-ambulatory patients requires stretchers, wheelchairs, and medical equipment transport - coordination with hospital staff is essential. Fire department must be notified for standby and to assist with evacuation. The leak source must be identified - recent utility work suggests third-party damage. The pipeline and other utilities should be checked for integrity. If the pipeline is leaking, isolation valves must be closed. Other utilities may also be sources - sewer gas, propane, or internal plumbing leaks should be considered. Gas migration pathways must be identified - storm drains, utility conduits, or soil may carry gas. The area around the hospital must be secured - no vehicles or ignition sources. Once leak is stopped, ventilation should clear the building before reoccupancy. Medical gas systems should be checked for contamination if natural gas entered. The hospital may need to divert incoming patients to other facilities. DOT reporting applies if the leak is from the pipeline. The third-party construction should be investigated for One Call compliance. Patient care continuity must be maintained through the incident. Documentation includes gas readings, evacuation actions, patient tracking, and leak cause determination.
abnormal_variants: Gas readings exceed UEL in parts of building
